"I've Tried Things Like This Before" — Historical Identity Objection

This objection sounds like product skepticism. It's actually about the prospect's history with themselves — and that's a completely different problem to solve.

What This Objection Actually Is

When a prospect says "I've tried things like this before and it didn't work," they are making an identity statement about their own history. The statement is: "I am someone who has tried and not succeeded."

The surface objection is about your program. The real objection is about them. They've compiled evidence that investing in this category of thing leads to disappointment — and they're applying that evidence to you before you've even had a chance to differentiate.

Why This Is a Historical Identity Objection

The prospect isn't just telling you about past programs. They're telling you about past versions of themselves. "I tried and it didn't work" carries a subtext of "I tried and I didn't work." They may not be aware of this — but it's there. And it makes this objection resistant to logical rebuttal, because the logic they're defending is emotional.

"You cannot argue someone out of their own history. But you can help them see that their history isn't the only possible future."

— JJ Hernandez

Why Arguing With Past Experience Always Fails

The moment you try to prove that your program is better than what they've tried before, you've made a critical error: you've positioned the conversation as your program vs. their experience. Their experience wins every time. It's personal. It's lived. It's real to them in a way that your pitch can never be.

When a rep says "but we're totally different" after someone says they've been burned before, the prospect hears: "Ignore your own experience and trust me instead." That is not a persuasive argument. It is a red flag.

What the Rep Does Wrong What Actually Works
Immediately defends the program: "We're different though." Validates the experience first: "That makes total sense. Tell me more about what happened."
Lists differentiators before understanding what failed. Asks what made the past experience not work — and uses the answer to differentiate.
Gets defensive about being compared to other programs. Welcomes the comparison — shows curiosity, not insecurity.
Talks about the program before talking about the person. Leads with understanding the prospect's past experience so the differentiation is precise.

The Move — Validate, Then Separate

01
Step One
Validate the Past Experience
Before you say anything about your program, acknowledge what they went through. Not in a corporate "I'm so sorry to hear that" way — genuinely. "That's a real thing — there's a lot of stuff in this space that overpromises. Tell me what that was like."
Why it works: Validation drops the prospect's guard. They're expecting you to defend yourself. When you don't — when you actually ask about their experience with curiosity — the dynamic shifts. You become the first person in this conversation who isn't selling past their pain.
02
Step Two
Ask What Made Those Experiences Not Work
The key question: "What made those experiences not work for you?" This question does two things simultaneously: it gets you the actual failure points from their past, and it gives you a specific differentiation target. Instead of defending your program generically, you get to address the exact thing that failed them before.
Prospect might say: "It was all generic content, no personal attention." Now you know exactly what to differentiate on. You're not defending against a vague comparison — you're addressing the specific failure they named.
03
Step Three
Separate This From That — Precisely
Now differentiate — but using their words, not yours. "The thing you described — generic content with no feedback loop — is exactly what this isn't. The whole model is built around personal attention because we saw what happened when that element was missing." You're not saying "we're different." You're showing them the specific difference that addresses the specific failure they described.
The key: never separate generically. "We're unique" means nothing. "The thing that failed you in that experience is the thing we built this around solving" is surgical and credible.

Call Exchange — Right vs. Wrong Response

VERSION 1 — Rep Who Defends the Program Immediately
Prospect
"I've actually invested in programs like this before and honestly didn't see the results I was promised. So I'm pretty skeptical."
Rep
"Yeah we hear that a lot — there's definitely a lot of bad programs out there. But we're different. We have a money-back guarantee, the coaching is highly personalized, and JJ has worked with Iman Gadzhi's team so the credibility is real. We get actual results."
Immediately pivoted to defending. Didn't ask about the past experience. Didn't validate. The prospect now has to evaluate a list of claims against their lived experience. Their experience wins.
Prospect
"Yeah... I've heard that before too though. I'm just not sure."
Exact outcome: the defense sounds identical to what every other program said before the prospect was disappointed.
VERSION 2 — Rep Who Validates and Separates Precisely
Prospect
"I've actually invested in programs like this before and honestly didn't see the results I was promised. So I'm pretty skeptical."
Rep
"That's completely fair. And honestly — there's a lot in this space that deserves to be skeptical of. What were those experiences like? What made them not work for you?"
Validates. Doesn't defend. Asks about the past experience specifically. The prospect was expecting a pitch. They got curiosity instead.
Prospect
"It was basically a course with a bunch of videos. Once I paid I never heard from anyone. And the tactics were very scripted — like it didn't account for how actual conversations go."
Rep
"Okay — I want to be specific with you because that matters. What you described — a course model where you pay and disappear, scripted tactics that break down in real conversations — that's actually the opposite of how this is structured. There are no scripts here. You get weekly 1-on-1 coaching, group call reviews of your actual calls, and a community where you have direct access to coaches. The people who built this program built it because they were frustrated by exactly what you described."
Differentiates using their exact words. Not generic. Not defensive. Addresses the specific failures they named. The contrast is clear without dismissing their experience.
Lesson 9 · Section 6

"What If It Doesn't Work?" — The Fear of Failure Objection

The prospect who asks "what if it doesn't work?" is not being pessimistic. They've already invested emotionally — they want this to work. The fear is of disappointment, not of failure itself.

What's Really Happening When They Ask This

This is actually a good sign. A prospect who asks "what if it doesn't work?" has already emotionally invested in this working. They can see themselves in it. They can see the outcome. That's why they're afraid of not getting it.

A prospect who doesn't care whether it works doesn't ask this question. They just say "I'm not interested." The fear of failure is the evidence of desire. Handle it accordingly.

The Two Layers

Surface layer: "Does this program deliver results?" This can be addressed with the guarantee, proof, and track record.

Deeper layer: "What if I don't work out? What if I put in everything and I'm still the one who doesn't get there?" This is the real fear — and it's about them, not the program. It requires a different response than the guarantee.

The Core Reframe — Define "Doesn't Work" Together

The question to ask: "Let's talk about what 'doesn't work' would actually mean for you — what would that look like?"

This does something important: it forces the prospect to define failure. Most of the time, the definition of failure they name is something you can address, quantify, or guarantee around. You can't work with a vague fear. You can work with a specific scenario.

What Happens When You Ask

Most prospects will say something like: "I'd put in the time and still be at the same number" or "I'd spend the money and not get a return on it." These are specific fears with specific answers: the guarantee addresses financial risk, the track record addresses results, and your coaching accountability addresses effort-without-outcome.

Occasionally a prospect will say "I'd feel like a failure." That's the deeper layer. Address it directly and with respect — "That's a real concern. Let me tell you something about how we approach the people in this program when they hit walls."

How to Use the Guarantee — and When Not To Lead With It

The mistake: Leading with the guarantee as the first response to this objection. When you open with "we have a money-back guarantee" before you understand what they're actually afraid of, it sounds like a standard pitch response. It doesn't address the fear — it just reduces the financial risk, which may not be the primary concern.

Use the Guarantee as a Landing Point, Not a Lead

First: ask what "doesn't work" means to them. Second: address the specific fear they name. Third: use the guarantee to close the loop on financial risk after the emotional concern has been heard and addressed.

"So based on what you described — you'd want to make sure you're not investing money you won't see again. The guarantee is specifically built for that. If you put in the work and don't make your investment back in 90 days, you get a full refund. That's not a marketing line — we stand behind it."

The Guarantee as a Statement About the Program, Not Just Risk Mitigation

Frame the guarantee not just as financial protection but as a signal of confidence: "The reason we offer a guarantee is because we know what happens when people actually go through this program. A guarantee only makes financial sense for a company that doesn't expect to give refunds. We don't."

Separating Effort From Outcome — When the Fear Is About Themselves

When the Deeper Fear Surfaces

If the prospect's fear is about their own ability to execute — "what if I put in the work and I'm still not good enough" — this is Barrier Three from Lesson 3. They're protecting against the pain of maximum effort with no result. Address it from a place of earned authority, not reassurance.

"The people who ask that question are the ones most likely to get results. Because the ones who don't care about failing don't actually try hard enough to get them. The fact that you're asking this tells me you'd put in the work. And this program is built for people who put in the work."

The Accountability Frame

If effort and follow-through is the concern: "Here's what you should know — this isn't a program where you're on your own. You have weekly 1-on-1 coaching specifically to catch people when they're stuck, before the stagnation becomes a problem. If you hit a wall, there's a person who is specifically there to help you through it. You're not doing this alone."

Call Exchange — Right vs. Wrong Response

VERSION 1 — Rep Who Leads With the Guarantee
Prospect
"I guess my concern is — what if I go through this and it doesn't work out? What if I just don't get the results?"
Rep
"That's why we have our money-back guarantee. If you put in the work and don't get results in 90 days, we refund you in full. So honestly the risk is pretty minimal."
Went straight to financial risk mitigation. Didn't ask what "doesn't work" means to the prospect. Didn't address the emotional layer. The prospect wanted to feel heard — they got a policy.
Prospect
"Yeah... okay. I still just need to think about it."
The guarantee didn't close the real fear. The real fear was never surfaced.
VERSION 2 — Rep Who Surfaces and Addresses the Real Fear
Prospect
"I guess my concern is — what if I go through this and it doesn't work out? What if I just don't get the results?"
Rep
"That's worth talking through. What does 'doesn't work' look like for you — specifically? Like what's the scenario you're imagining?"
Asks the defining question. Forces the fear to become concrete before addressing it.
Prospect
"I guess... I'd spend the money and still be stuck at the same income level. Like I'd put in the effort and nothing would actually change."
Rep
"Okay — two things. First, the fact that you said 'put in the effort' tells me something. The people who don't get results from this program are the ones who don't engage. You're already thinking about putting in the work — that's the profile of someone who gets results here. Second — to address the financial side directly — we guarantee that you'll make your investment back in 90 days or we refund you. That exists specifically for the scenario you just described."
Addresses both layers: the personal fear about effort and the financial fear about return. In the right order — emotion first, then logistics. The guarantee lands as closure, not as a deflection.
Lesson 10 · Section 6

Energy Shift Detection — "I Feel Like the Energy Shifted. Am I Wrong?"

The most dangerous thing that happens on a call is invisible — the moment the prospect disconnects emotionally. Most reps miss it entirely. Elite reps catch it in real time and address it directly.

What an Energy Shift Is

The Definition

An energy shift is the moment when a prospect's internal state changes from open to closed — without them saying anything. The call was going well, there was flow, the prospect was engaged. And then something changed. They got shorter. Their answers got less personal. The questions they were asking stopped.

Most reps don't notice it until much later — usually at the stall or the close. By then, whatever caused the shift has had time to calcify into a "no" or a "let me think about it."

The rep who catches the shift in real time and names it can recover the call. The rep who doesn't catch it will lose the call to a stall they never understood.

Reading Shifts in Real Time

These are the behavioral signals that tell you an energy shift has happened. You're listening for changes — not absolute states.

  • !
    Answers get shorter. They were giving you full sentences, real context. Now they're giving you one or two words. "Yeah." "Right." "Sure." The volunteering has stopped. They're responding to survive the call, not to engage with it.
  • !
    Questions stop. An engaged prospect asks questions. When the questions stop and the prospect is only answering yours, they've withdrawn. The curiosity died. Something made them pull back.
  • !
    Pace changes. They were matching your energy. Now they're slower, more clipped. Or they've started rushing — trying to get to the end of the call. Both are shifts. Disengagement can look like withdrawal or like urgency to end.
  • !
    Vocabulary becomes formal. Early in the call they were casual, natural. Now they're using formal language: "I appreciate you walking me through this," "This is certainly interesting." Formality is emotional distance.
  • !
    They stop using your name or referring back to what you said. Engaged prospects reference things from earlier in the call. When they stop doing that, they've disconnected from the continuity of the conversation.

Why Naming the Shift Is the Move

What most reps do when they sense a shift: Push harder. Increase enthusiasm. Talk more. Try to re-engage through volume or energy. This is exactly wrong. It's the equivalent of someone going quiet at a dinner table and you responding by telling more jokes. The person who was quiet needed to be asked what was on their mind — not entertained back into engagement.

The move is to name the shift directly: "I feel like something shifted for you a few minutes ago — what's going on?"

Or: "I want to check in — you seem like something landed differently than I expected. Did something I said not sit right?"

Naming the shift does two things. First, it tells the prospect that you're actually paying attention to them, not just to your pitch. Second, it creates an opening for them to tell you the truth — which is the only thing that can actually recover the call.

Recovering a Call That's Gone Cold

01
Recovery Step One
Stop What You're Doing
As soon as you notice the shift, stop. Don't finish your sentence if you don't need to. Don't finish the point you were making. The call has already moved away from the content — continuing to deliver it is just talking into silence. Interrupt yourself if necessary.
"Actually — before I go further — I want to check in. It feels like something shifted. What's going on?"
02
Recovery Step Two
Hold the Silence After Naming It
You've named the shift. Now wait. Don't soften it, don't add qualifiers, don't re-explain. The prospect needs space to respond. The silence is not awkward — it's necessary. You're creating room for the real conversation to start. Hold it for as long as it takes.
If they say "no, no, everything's fine" — that's information. A prospect who was fine wouldn't need to clarify that they're fine. Gently push: "Okay — I'm not trying to make this weird, I just noticed the energy change and want to make sure we're actually talking about what matters to you."
03
Recovery Step Three
Respond to What's Real, Not What They Said
Once they surface what actually changed for them — maybe something you said landed wrong, maybe the price registered, maybe a past experience came to mind — respond to that specifically. Don't go back to the pitch. The pitch is suspended until the real thing gets addressed. The call can only be recovered by going through the real concern, not around it.

Call Exchange — Ignoring vs. Naming the Shift

VERSION 1 — Rep Who Ignores the Shift and Keeps Pitching
Rep
"So the job board has 60 to 80 new postings per week, all vetted companies, and we guarantee placement within the first month. Pretty powerful, right?"
Prospect
"Yeah."
One word. Was giving full answers before. Something shifted.
Rep
"And then the group calls — we have seven per week covering every area of the sales process. Mock calls, B2B strategy, inner circle for the advanced reps. So there's a ton of live learning opportunities on top of the 1-on-1 work."
Rep doesn't notice — or notices and pushes through anyway. Keeps adding information to a prospect who has already disengaged. The call is dying.
Prospect
"Yeah, sounds good. I'll need to think about it and get back to you."
Stall. The call is over. Rep never found out what caused the shift — and will never close this deal.
VERSION 2 — Rep Who Names the Shift and Recovers
Rep
"So the job board has 60 to 80 new postings per week, all vetted companies, and we guarantee placement within the first month. Pretty powerful, right?"
Prospect
"Yeah."
Rep
[Pauses] "Actually — hold on. I feel like something shifted for you just now. What's going on?"
Stops the pitch. Names the shift directly. Doesn't pretend not to notice.
Prospect
"I mean... honestly, when you said the price earlier I think it just kind of registered. It's more than I was expecting."
The real thing. The price hit at some point in the call and they've been processing it since. The rep caught it before it became a ghost.
Rep
"I'm glad you said that. Let's talk about it directly — because if that's what's in your head, nothing I say about the program is landing the way it should. Tell me more about where you're at with the investment."
Validates. Pauses the pitch. Opens the real conversation. This is what recovery looks like — addressing what's real before getting back to the content.
Lesson 11 · Section 6

The Cost Reframe — "Is It Truly Impossible, or Not Fully Confident Yet?"

Most money objections are certainty problems wearing financial language. The cost reframe separates the two — and it does it in a single question that changes everything.

The Core Distinction

There is a fundamental difference between "I truly cannot afford this" and "I'm not confident enough to justify spending this."

They sound identical. The prospect uses similar language for both. The treatment is completely different. Treating a confidence gap as a financial impossibility — or vice versa — is one of the most common reasons reps lose deals they should have closed.

Why the Distinction Matters

"Truly can't" means the money is not accessible. No amount of confidence-building changes this. Your conversation is about financing options and whether this is actually the right time.

"Not confident enough" means the money exists but the prospect's certainty in the outcome doesn't yet justify accessing it. No payment plan changes this. Your conversation is about building certainty — proof, specifics, the guarantee, comparable outcomes.

If you offer payment plans to someone with a confidence gap, they'll say "let me think about it" about the smaller number. You didn't solve anything — you just made the problem cheaper.

The Reframe Question

"If we could guarantee you'd make your investment back in 90 days, would the money still be a hard stop?"

This is the cost reframe. It's a diagnostic question — and the answer tells you exactly which conversation to have.

If They Say Yes — "It Would Still Be a Hard Stop"

The money is genuinely not accessible. This is real. Respect it. Your next conversation is about whether the timing is actually wrong, whether financing changes anything, or whether this is a no for right now — not forever. Don't push on certainty. The certainty would have closed it if the money were real.

If They Hesitate or Say No — "Well, If You Could Guarantee It..."

This is a certainty gap. The money exists. The confidence doesn't. Now you're in the right conversation — and you have a very direct path to closing it. You can say: "So the money isn't the actual blocker — it's whether you believe this will work for you. Let's talk about that specifically."

What to Do With the Answer

A
Path A — Confidence Gap
Build Certainty, Then Use the Guarantee
Name the guarantee explicitly — not as a marketing item, but as the direct answer to their concern: "You said if we could guarantee the return, the money isn't the issue. We do guarantee it — in writing. If you put in the work and you don't make your investment back in 90 days, we refund you in full. That's the answer to what you just told me."
Then add a real comparable outcome: "And let me tell you about someone who started in a situation very close to yours. Here's where they were, here's where they went, and here's how long it took." Make it specific. Certainty comes from specifics, not claims.
B
Path B — True Financial Constraint
Restructure or Qualify the Timing
If the money genuinely isn't there, offer the payment structure options — and be specific about what they require. Don't make the program sound cheap. Frame the payment option as a structure for accessing the same outcome, not as a discount. If no structure works, be honest: "It sounds like this might be better timing a bit later. Let's be real about when that window opens and what needs to be different."
The mistake here is trying to push a sale through financial constraints that are real. That creates a buyer who can't afford what they committed to — and that's a refund and a bad outcome for everyone.

The Reframe in Context — Applying It Right

What the Prospect Says What to Ask What the Answer Reveals
"I don't have the money right now." "If we guaranteed the return, would it still be a hard stop?" Yes = true constraint. Hesitation/No = certainty gap.
"It's more than I was expecting to spend." "Is the issue the number itself, or more about what you'd get back from it?" The number = value question. What you'd get back = certainty gap.
"I'd need to move some things around to make it work." "What would make moving things around feel worth it to you?" They're not saying no — they're telling you what they'd need to believe. Find out what that is.
"I've spent money on things that didn't work before." "So the real concern is certainty about the outcome, not the number itself?" Almost always yes. This is a certainty gap and a historical identity objection combined.

Call Exchange — The Cost Reframe Applied

EXCHANGE — Rep Who Uses the Reframe Correctly
Prospect
"The money is honestly the main thing holding me back. I just don't know if I can swing it right now."
Rep
"I want to ask you something directly. If we could guarantee you'd make your investment back within 90 days — would the money still be a hard stop, or is it more about whether you're confident enough in the outcome to commit to it?"
The cost reframe question. Diagnostic, not pressuring. Creates two paths.
Prospect
"I mean... if you could actually guarantee that, it would change things. But I've heard 'we guarantee results' from a lot of people."
Certainty gap. The money exists. The confidence in the guarantee's reality doesn't. The prospect is saying: prove the guarantee is real.
Rep
"That's fair — so the real issue isn't that the money can't happen. It's that you don't yet believe the guarantee is real. I respect that. Let me address it specifically. We've had 549 students come through. One refund — medical emergency, nothing to do with results. We've never been asked for a refund because someone didn't get results and we refused. The reason we offer the guarantee is because we know what happens when people actually go through this."
Reframes from "money" to "certainty" — because the prospect just confirmed that's the real issue. Then addresses the certainty gap with specifics: 549 students, 1 refund, the reason for the guarantee. Logical and credible, not defensive.
Lesson 12 · Section 6

Pattern Interrupting Stalls Before They Happen

Most reps wait for stalls to arrive, then try to recover. Elite reps see them coming and change the direction before the stall has time to form.

The Premise

Stalls are predictable. "I need to think about it," "let me talk to my spouse," "can you send me something?" — every rep has heard these so many times they should be able to prevent them. But most reps don't. They wait for the stall, then scramble.

Pattern interrupting is the practice of redirecting the conversation before the stall takes shape. Not by rushing to close, but by addressing the underlying condition that was going to create the stall in the first place.

The stall is a symptom. The pattern interrupt treats the cause.

What Creates Stalls

Stalls come from three places:

1. Uncertainty — they're not sure the program works or works for them specifically. They need more certainty before they can decide.

2. Identity misalignment — some part of them doesn't see themselves as "someone who does this." The stall is them retreating to a familiar identity.

3. Permission — they feel like they need someone else's buy-in (spouse, partner, mentor) before they can feel okay making the decision.

If you address all three of these during discovery and pitch — before the close — the stall either doesn't appear or appears in a weakened form that's easy to handle.

Pattern Interrupt Seeding — Lines to Plant Early

Seed these during the call — in discovery, during pitch, or at transition. Not at the close. By the time you're closing, these should already be in place.

Seed 1 — Certainty interrupt (use mid-pitch)
"I want to be upfront — a lot of people come into this call wanting to make sure it works before they commit. I'd rather you not join if you're not certain. So as we go through this, tell me what you're not seeing yet."
Why it works: Names their uncertainty, gives them permission to voice it now instead of at the close, positions you as the opposite of pushy.
Seed 2 — Identity interrupt (use after discovery)
"Can I ask you something? You mentioned you're serious about this. When you imagine the version of you who's actually at $20k a month — is there any part of you that still isn't sure that person is you?"
Why it works: Surfaces the identity doubt early. They address it now instead of it festering into a "let me think about it."
Seed 3 — Spouse/partner interrupt (use in discovery if they mention a partner)
"Is this something your [spouse/partner] knows you're looking at? I ask because decisions like this are better made together, and I want to make sure whoever matters to you is at least in the loop."
Why it works: Surfaces the spouse objection early while you can address it, instead of it appearing as a stall at the end of the call.
Seed 4 — Decision pattern interrupt (use before price)
"Before I show you the investment — I want to ask: when you make decisions like this, do you generally know pretty quickly, or do you need time to process?"
Why it works: Gets them to name their decision pattern. If they say "I need time," you can explore what that means. If they say "I generally know quickly," you can hold them to that.

The Pattern Interrupt in Action

Rep who didn't seed — standard stall at close
RepSo the investment is $7,500. What do you think?
ProspectI love it. I just want to think about it and talk to my wife and maybe get back to you next week.
RepOf course, totally makes sense. I'll follow up Monday!
Three stalls hit at once. Rep never seeded. Call is essentially over.
Rep who seeded — stalls addressed before they formed
Rep[Early in call] Is your wife aware you're looking at this? I ask because these decisions are better with both people understanding what's happening.
ProspectYeah she knows I'm on a call about sales training. She's supportive but probably wants to know more.
RepMakes sense. If she had one concern, what do you think it would be?
ProspectProbably just if it's actually going to work.
RepThat's fair. Let's make sure we actually address that today so you're not carrying that uncertainty into a decision.
Spouse concern surfaced and addressed in discovery. Can't become a close-time stall now.

Timing Is Everything

Pattern interrupt seeds must go in early — not right before the close. If you try to pattern-interrupt at the close, it reads as manipulation. It needs to happen during discovery or pitch, when the context is exploratory and you're genuinely trying to help them make a clear decision.

The goal is never to prevent the prospect from thinking or involving their partner. The goal is to do that work now, together, on this call — so they're not doing it alone in an information vacuum after you hang up.

Lesson 13 · Section 6

Handling Buyer's Remorse

The sale doesn't end when they say yes. The 24-48 hours after the close is when doubt peaks. What you do at the moment of close determines whether the commitment holds.

What Buyer's Remorse Actually Is

Buyer's remorse is the discomfort that follows a big decision. It's not a sign the person made the wrong choice — it's a neurological response to commitment. The brain second-guesses large decisions as a protective mechanism.

It peaks in the first 24-48 hours after the call. This is the window where chargebacks happen, texts come in saying "actually I need to cancel," and deals that seemed solid dissolve.

The antidote is not a better close. It's what you say at the moment of close and in the immediate follow-up. You're not fighting remorse after it arrives — you're inoculating against it while you're still on the phone.

The Mental Vaccine — Lines at the Moment of Close

Use these at the close, once they've committed — not before. The goal is to reinforce the decision they just made, not the program they just bought.

Vaccine 1 — Reinforce the decision, not the product
"I want to say something. A lot of people get off a call like this and second-guess themselves for the next 48 hours. I want you to know — the decision you just made wasn't about a program. It was about deciding you're serious. That part doesn't change."
Why: Separates the decision from the price. The decision was about identity. Identity is harder to walk back than a financial commitment.
Vaccine 2 — Name the doubt before it hits
"In the next day or two, you might get a thought that says 'wait, did I just make the right call?' That thought is normal. It shows up for basically everyone who makes a serious investment in themselves. When it does — come back to why you made this decision today, not the number."
Why: Naming the doubt before it arrives defuses it when it does. The prospect expects it now instead of being caught off guard by it.
Vaccine 3 — Lock in the next step immediately
"Here's what's happening right now: [onboarding step, first call date, Slack invite]. The fastest way to feel good about this decision is to start taking action on it today."
Why: Momentum is the antidote to remorse. Getting them into action immediately anchors them in the decision through behavior, not just emotion.

Post-Close Follow-Up Protocol

  • Same day (within 2 hours): Send a brief, warm message — not a sales message. "Fired up to have you in. Your first [session/call] is [date]. Here's what to expect." Makes the program feel real immediately.
  • Evening of day 1: If you sense any hesitation during the call, or if it was a big decision for them — a brief check-in. "How are you feeling? Any questions since we talked?" Shows you care about the person, not just the transaction.
  • Day 2-3: Introduce them to the community or next step. The longer they wait to engage, the more remorse builds. Action is the cure.

If Remorse Shows Up Anyway

If you get the "I changed my mind" message: Don't panic. Don't immediately defend or justify. Ask one question first: "What specifically shifted for you since we talked?" The answer tells you whether this is fixable (usually it is) or whether they were never fully bought in (and you need to let them go with grace).

  • Don't argue the value. Arguing about why they should stay sounds like you need the money. It accelerates the exit.
  • Get specific. "What specifically is the concern?" — vague remorse is fixable. A specific concern is a specific conversation.
  • Bring them back to the decision. "You told me X was the reason you were serious about this. Is that still true?" Remind them of their own words from discovery.
  • Know when to release. Some people aren't ready. Holding someone in a program they've mentally left damages the community and your reputation. Release them cleanly when that's the right call.
S7
Sales Psychology
Lesson 1 · Section 7

How Buying Decisions Actually Happen

System 1 vs. System 2. Understanding which brain makes the call — and which one writes the justification after the fact — changes everything about how you sell.

The Two Systems

Daniel Kahneman — Thinking, Fast and Slow

Psychologist Daniel Kahneman spent decades studying how humans make decisions. His research produced one of the most important frameworks in behavioral science: the two-system model of thought.

It's not complicated. You have two modes of processing. System 1 runs automatically — fast, emotional, pattern-based. System 2 runs deliberately — slow, logical, effortful. They operate simultaneously, but they serve very different functions.

Dimension System 1 — Fast Brain System 2 — Slow Brain
Speed Instantaneous Slow, requires effort
Mode Automatic, involuntary Deliberate, voluntary
Fuel Pattern recognition, emotion, gut feel Logic, analysis, comparison
Role in buying Makes the actual decision Rationalizes it afterward

The uncomfortable truth: buying decisions are made by System 1. System 2 believes it's in charge, but it almost never is. The research shows that people make the emotional decision first — within seconds — and then use logical reasoning to justify what they've already decided. System 2 is the press secretary. System 1 is the president.

What This Means for Sales

The Rational Pitch Problem

Most salespeople are trained to pitch to System 2. Features, benefits, ROI calculations, logical comparisons. The problem is that System 2 isn't where the decision lives. You can win the logical argument completely and still lose the sale.

The prospect's System 2 is listening to your pitch and constructing counter-arguments, objections, and reasons to wait. Meanwhile, their System 1 has already formed an impression of you — and that impression is running the show.

If the prospect doesn't feel safe with you, doesn't feel like you understand them, doesn't feel genuine curiosity from your side — their System 1 will reject the deal regardless of how airtight the logical case is. You can't logic your way past a bad emotional read.

System 1 Inputs That Drive Buying

Your tone of voice. Your pacing. Whether you sound like you need the sale. How quickly you listen vs. how quickly you talk. Whether you feel curious or transactional. Whether the prospect feels seen or processed.

None of these are in your pitch deck. All of them are in your presence on the call.

The Real Decision Sequence

01
System 1 — First 90 Seconds
Gut Read of the Rep
Before a single feature is mentioned, the prospect's fast brain has assessed: do I trust this person? Are they confident without being pushy? Do they seem like they're here to help or here to sell? This read happens faster than conscious thought and shapes everything that follows.
02
System 1 — Discovery
Emotional Activation
Good discovery activates System 1 by making the pain vivid and the desired future vivid. When a prospect is actually feeling the cost of their current situation — not just describing it — their fast brain is primed to want a solution. The pitch lands on warm ground instead of cold.
03
System 1 — The Moment of Decision
The Buy Decision
The actual decision — yes or no — is made emotionally, usually before the price conversation even happens. By the time price comes up, System 1 has already voted. The price conversation is where System 2 negotiates the terms of what System 1 already decided.
04
System 2 — Post-Decision
The Justification
After deciding yes, System 2 constructs the logical case. "The ROI makes sense, the timing is right, the program is legitimate." This is why your proof points matter — not to drive the decision, but to give System 2 what it needs to feel good about the decision System 1 already made.

Practical Application

  • ✓
    Win the emotional brain first. Your first job on every call is to create safety, trust, and genuine curiosity. Before you pitch anything, make sure the prospect's System 1 has a positive read on you.
  • ✓
    Make pain vivid, not just described. "I'm not happy with my numbers" is a System 2 statement. "I watched another month go by and I'm still in the same place I was a year ago, and it's starting to feel like this is just who I am" is System 1 activated. Slow down in discovery and get the real feeling on the surface.
  • ✓
    Let System 2 do its job. Testimonials, track record, money-back guarantee, job placement data — these aren't for convincing. They're for confirming. Give System 2 what it needs to feel smart about the decision. Present proof after emotional buy-in, not instead of it.
  • ✗
    Don't lead with logic. Opening with features, program structure, or ROI calculations before you've created emotional connection is like pitching to an audience that isn't there yet. You've got a logical framework and a disconnected prospect. Nothing will land.

Call Exchange

The System 1 vs. System 2 Mistake — Live on a Call
Prospect
"I want to understand what makes this different from other sales programs. Give me the breakdown."
Rep (System 2 pitch)
"Sure — so we have 7 weekly group calls, weekly 1-on-1s, a job board with 60 to 80 vetted companies, and a money-back guarantee. The curriculum covers psychology, setting, PACE method, and scriptless selling. It's 12 weeks at $7,500."
All System 2 content. Factually accurate. Emotionally empty. Prospect's System 1 has nothing to hold onto.
Prospect
"Okay... I'd want to think about it and compare some options."
Same Question — Answered to System 1 First
Prospect
"I want to understand what makes this different from other sales programs. Give me the breakdown."
Rep
"I'll get there. But first — what has every other program you've looked at been missing? Because if I just pitch you a list of features, I'm doing the same thing they did. What would actually move the needle for you?"
Redirects to System 1. Gets the prospect emotionally engaged in articulating what they actually need. Now when the breakdown comes, it lands on primed ground.
Prospect
"Honestly, I need someone who's going to actually hold me accountable. Not just content I never finish."
Rep
"That's exactly what this is built around. Here's what accountability actually looks like in this program — not in theory, in practice..."
Now the System 2 content (the breakdown) maps directly to what System 1 just said it needed. The emotional brain says yes. The logical brain confirms it.
Lesson 2 · Section 7

The Halo Effect

Your first 90 seconds determine the lens through which the prospect experiences everything else on the call. Get the opening right and the whole call is easier. Get it wrong and you're fighting uphill the entire time.

What the Halo Effect Is

The Psychology

The Halo Effect is the cognitive bias where one strong positive impression causes us to view everything else about a person or experience more favorably. One negative first impression does the reverse — a "reverse halo" or horn effect — where subsequent information gets filtered through a skeptical lens.

Psychologist Edward Thorndike identified it in the 1920s. Solomon Asch expanded on it. Kahneman describes it as one of System 1's most powerful shortcuts: instead of evaluating each piece of information independently, we let one strong data point color the whole picture.

In a sales call context: the halo you create in the first 90 seconds is the lens through which every single thing you say afterward will be interpreted. Strong opening — your objections are received as curiosity. Weak opening — your best points are received as desperation.

What Creates a Strong Positive Halo

The Four Signals

A strong opening halo isn't about what you say. It's about four things the prospect's System 1 reads in the first 90 seconds — none of which are in your script.

Signal Negative Halo Positive Halo
Tone Eager, slightly high-pitched, over-friendly — reads as needy Calm, grounded, unhurried — reads as certain
Pace Fast, steamrolling into the agenda — reads as nervous Deliberate pauses, comfortable silences — reads as confident
Opening question "Tell me a little bit about yourself" — generic, forgettable Specific to them, shows you prepared — reads as someone who cares
Energy Performed enthusiasm — "This is awesome!" — reads as fake Genuine interest without performance — reads as real
The Non-Needy Frame

The single most powerful halo signal is non-neediness. When the prospect senses you do not need this particular call to go a certain way, they relax. Their guard comes down. They start treating the conversation like a dialogue between two people with equal standing — which is exactly the frame you want.

Neediness broadcasts in your opening before you say a word: in how quickly you start talking, how you fill silence, how enthusiastic you sound about getting through your agenda.

What Creates a Negative Halo

Over-enthusiasm kills the halo. The rep who opens with "I'm so excited to talk with you today!" has already told the prospect they care too much about this. The prospect's System 1 reads it correctly: this person needs something from me. Guard goes up.

The unprepared opener. "So, tell me a little about yourself and what you're looking for." The prospect just got the same question they've been asked by every other rep they've ever talked to. The halo is: generic. Not someone worth paying $7,500 to.

Rushing into the agenda. Starting a call with "Great, so I'll walk you through what we cover, then we'll talk about fit, and then we can look at next steps..." signals that this is a process you're running, not a conversation you're having. The prospect feels processed, not seen.

The halo is hard to reverse. A negative first impression requires roughly 8 positive interactions to fully override, according to behavioral research. You can recover from a bad opening — but it costs you energy, credibility, and momentum you need for the close. Better to get it right the first time.

How to Recover if the Opening Missed

The Pattern Interrupt

If you feel the call starting to slip — the prospect is short with answers, the energy is flat, they seem checked out — you can interrupt the pattern. Not with a tactic, with honesty.

"Let me start over. I want to actually understand what's going on for you — not run through a process. What would make this call actually worth your time today?"

This works because it demonstrates the exact quality that creates a positive halo: not needing the call to go a specific way. The moment you give them permission to redirect, you've become the most trustworthy person who's ever gotten on a call with them.

Call Exchange — The First 90 Seconds

VERSION 1 — Negative Halo Opening
Rep
"Hey [name]! So great to connect — I've been looking forward to this call all day. So I'll take you through a little bit about what we do, we'll talk about where you're at and see if there's a fit, and then we'll figure out next steps from there. Sound good?"
Over-enthusiastic. Agenda-first. "Sound good?" seeks permission — signals insecurity. The prospect now knows this rep wants something from them.
Prospect
"Sure, yeah."
Flat. Non-committal. The halo is already negative. Guard is up.
VERSION 2 — Positive Halo Opening
Rep
"Hey [name] — good to have you on. Before I get into anything, I read through what you shared. You mentioned you've been in sales for about two years and you're stuck around the $8-10K mark. What's been the biggest thing getting in the way?"
No over-enthusiasm. No agenda list. Goes straight to them. Shows prep. Opens with a specific, relevant question. The prospect's System 1 reads: this person is here for me, not for a sale.
Prospect
"Honestly it's been consistency — I'll have a great week and then I can't figure out how to repeat it."
The prospect is engaged. They're actually answering. The halo is positive. The rest of the call will be filtered through: this rep gets it.

Pre-Call Halo Checklist

  • ✓
    Know one specific thing about this prospect before you dial. Use it in your opening question.
  • ✓
    Slow your pace by 20% in the first 60 seconds. Feel the urge to rush. Do the opposite.
  • ✓
    No agenda-listing in the opening. Let the conversation direct itself. Show you can hold space without a script.
  • ✗
    No performance enthusiasm. "So excited to talk to you" is a halo killer. Replace it with calm presence.
  • ✗
    No "sound good?" seeking approval. Make statements. Ask real questions. Don't seek permission to have a conversation.
Lesson 3 · Section 7

Anchoring

The first number stated in any negotiation becomes the reference point for everything that follows. Whoever sets the anchor owns the frame. Don't let that be the prospect.

What Anchoring Is

The Research

Anchoring is one of the most replicated findings in behavioral science. Kahneman and Tversky demonstrated it repeatedly: when people make numerical estimates, they are heavily influenced by the first number they hear, even when that number is arbitrary and irrelevant.

In one classic experiment, subjects who were first asked "Is the population of Turkey more or less than 65 million?" estimated a much higher population than those first asked "Is it more or less than 5 million?" The anchor shaped the estimate even when people knew it was random.

In sales: the first number stated in a conversation becomes the reference point against which all other numbers are measured. State value first. State price last. The price is measured against the anchor you set, not against the prospect's internal number.

Anchoring High Before Price

The Value Anchor

Before you reveal any number, you need to establish the value frame. The transformation value. The cost of the problem they have. The size of the opportunity they're missing. These create the high anchor against which your price will be evaluated.

If a rep doing $10K/month should be doing $30K — that's $20,000 a month in unrealized income. Over 12 months: $240,000 left on the table. That number is your real anchor. $7,500 against $240,000 is a completely different conversation than $7,500 as a standalone number.

The Cost of Inaction Anchor

Equally powerful: anchor to the cost of not acting. Not as a pressure tactic — as an honest frame. If the prospect stays where they are for another 12 months, what is that costing them? In income, in career trajectory, in the compounding effect of staying stuck? This cost is usually enormous, and naming it explicitly creates the anchor your price will be evaluated against.

Never Let the Prospect Anchor First

When a prospect asks "how much does it cost?" before you've established value — and you answer immediately — you've let them set the frame. Their internal number (often much lower than your price) becomes the anchor. Your price then lands as too high, even if it's objectively reasonable given the transformation.

If a prospect says "I was thinking something around $2,000" before you've revealed your price — that's a counter-anchor. Never confirm it. Respond by re-establishing value: "Before we talk about investment, I want to make sure we've looked at what the actual outcome is worth..."

The Anchoring Sequence in Practice

01
Anchor Step 1
Surface the Current Cost
In discovery, get specific numbers. Not "I want to earn more" — "What are you making right now per month? What would you need to be at to feel like you've actually broken through?" Get them to articulate the gap in dollar terms. These are their own numbers — and they become your anchor.
"You said you're at $9K right now and you want to be at $25K. That's a $16,000 a month difference. Over a year, that's close to $200K. Do you see that gap the same way I do?"
02
Anchor Step 2
Describe the Transformation Value
Before price, walk through what the outcome is actually worth. Placement on a $100K–$1M/month offer. Skills that compound over a career. The ability to close at a 40%+ rate vs. 15%. Frame the value in a way that makes the investment look small relative to what it produces.
"People who come out of this land roles paying $15K–$40K a month. Some significantly more. The skill set you develop here pays dividends for the rest of your career — not just the next 12 weeks."
03
Anchor Step 3
State the Price — Then Stop
With the anchors set, state the price simply and confidently. Then hold the silence. The price lands against the $200K gap, against the $30K/month future self, against the career-long compound effect. $7,500 against that backdrop is easy math. Let the prospect do the math in silence.
"The investment for 12 weeks is $7,500." [Full stop. Hold the silence.]

Call Exchange — Anchoring in Action

No Anchor — Price Lands in a Vacuum
Prospect
"So what's the price?"
Rep
"It's $7,500 for the 12-week program. We also have payment plans — you can split it into two payments of $3,750."
Price without anchor. No value frame. Payment plans introduced before they even respond — signals the rep expects price resistance. $7,500 lands against nothing. It feels expensive.
Anchored — Price Lands Against the Right Frame
Prospect
"So what's the price?"
Rep
"Before I get there — you said you're at $8K right now and you want to be at $25K. That's $17,000 a month you're not making, every single month this doesn't change. Over a year that's over $200,000 in income that's sitting on the table. The people who come out of this program land roles at $15K to $40K a month. So here's the question: what's it worth to close that gap inside of 90 days?"
Re-anchors to their own numbers. Frames the transformation value. Now the price will land against $200K and a $40K/month future.
Prospect
"I mean, it's worth a lot."
Rep
"The investment is $7,500."
Simple. Confident. Anchored. Now $7,500 feels like a fraction of the value. Hold the silence.
Lesson 4 · Section 7

Pre-Suasion

Influence doesn't begin when you pitch. It begins the moment the prospect's attention is directed — before you've said a word about your offer. What they're thinking about when they first hear you determines how they hear you.

What Pre-Suasion Is

Robert Cialdini — Pre-Suasion (2016)

In his follow-up to Influence, Cialdini introduced a concept that changes how you think about the entire structure of a sales call. Pre-suasion is the practice of directing attention in a way that makes the prospect more receptive to a specific message before that message is delivered.

The central insight: what people are thinking about at the moment of a pitch shapes how they receive that pitch. Get them thinking about safety and they'll evaluate your offer through a safety lens. Get them thinking about ambition and they'll evaluate it through an ambition lens. The frame is set before the pitch.

Pre-suasion is not manipulation. It's attention architecture. You decide which part of the prospect's experience you want activated before you speak to the thing your offer addresses. Every rep does this accidentally. Elite reps do it on purpose.

Where Pre-Suasion Lives on a Call

The Pre-Call Frame

The confirmation message you send before the call. The subject line. Whether you include anything substantive or just logistics. What the prospect reads in the 30 minutes before they get on with you sets their initial attention state. Most reps ignore this entirely.

A pre-call message that says "Looking forward to our call — I had a chance to review what you shared and I have some thoughts" primes the prospect to expect someone prepared and thoughtful. That's pre-suasion before the call even starts.

The Opening Questions

The first questions you ask direct attention. "Tell me about your background in sales" activates a different mental state than "When you think about where you want to be in your career, what does that actually look like?" The second question activates ambition and future-orientation — exactly the state you want someone in before hearing about a program that can accelerate their career.

The Discovery Sequence

The order of your discovery questions is pre-suasion architecture. Asking about pain before asking about goals means the prospect is primed by discomfort when they hear about solutions. Asking about the future first, then the gap between now and then, means they're in aspirational mode before the pain surfaces. Different orderings produce different emotional states at the moment of the pitch.

Engineering the Attention State

You Want Them Feeling Pre-Suasion Move
Frustrated by current reality Ask them to describe the pattern — not just the number. "Walk me through what a typical week looks like right now."
Excited about their future "Paint me a picture — what does your career look like 18 months from now if everything goes right?"
Aware of the cost of staying put "How long have you been in this spot? What's that cost you over that time?"
Confident in your credibility Name a specific result from someone in a similar situation before you pitch. Not a list — one vivid story.

The Pre-Suasion Call Architecture

01
Pre-Call
Confirmation Message
Send a brief, specific message that signals you prepared. One thing you noticed about their situation. This primes them to expect a different kind of conversation — not a sales process, but a genuine assessment.
02
Opening (0-3 min)
Activate the Ambitious Self
Your first question should activate the prospect's aspirational identity — who they want to be, not what's going wrong. Get them speaking from ambition first. That's the lens they'll evaluate your program through.
"Before we get into anything specific — what's the version of your career that you actually believe is possible for you?"
03
Discovery (3-20 min)
Surface the Gap
Now bring in the current reality. They're speaking from their ambitious self — now explore why they're not there yet. The frustration lands against the backdrop of the aspiration they just articulated. The gap feels real.
04
Pre-Pitch (before you present)
One Vivid Story
Before you present your program, name one person from a similar starting point with a specific outcome. Not "we have hundreds of success stories" — one person, one starting point, one result. That's the mental state you want them in when the pitch starts: "someone like me already did this."
"Before I walk you through what this actually looks like, let me tell you about someone who came in at almost exactly where you are..."

Call Exchange — Pre-Suasion in Opening

Opening Without Pre-Suasion Architecture
Rep
"Alright, so tell me a bit about your background and where you're at in sales right now."
Generic opener. Activates "current situation" frame — which is often frustration, confusion, or uncertainty. They begin the call in their pain state.
Opening WITH Pre-Suasion — Ambition First
Rep
"I want to start with something that most people skip. What does the career you actually believe is possible for you look like? Not the conservative version — the real one."
Activates aspiration and ambition. Prospect begins the call from their best self — the person who would make a bold investment decision. Everything from here is filtered through that lens.
Prospect
"Honestly? I think I could be making $30-40K a month in the next couple years. I know that sounds ambitious but I genuinely believe it."
Rep
"It doesn't sound ambitious — it sounds accurate. Tell me what's standing between where you are now and that number."
Now the pain surfaces — but it's framed against the aspiration they just voiced. The gap between $30-40K and their current reality is the pain. Much more motivating than starting with the pain cold.
Lesson 5 · Section 7

Priming Questions

Questions don't just gather information. They activate emotional states. The right question sequence gets the prospect into the buying state before you've said anything about your offer.

What Priming Questions Do

The Mechanism

Priming in psychology refers to the activation of specific associations in memory — where exposure to one stimulus influences the response to a subsequent stimulus. In a sales context, questions prime by directing the prospect's attention toward specific emotional states, memories, or identities.

When you ask someone "what would your life look like if this problem was completely solved?" — you've activated the part of their mind associated with relief, possibility, and desire. That emotional state is now active when you present your solution. Questions are the mechanism that opens the door.

The buying state isn't a place the prospect arrives naturally. You engineer it through question sequence. Pain vivid. Future vivid. Gap named. Solution clearly the bridge. That sequence doesn't happen by accident.

What the Buying State Actually Is

Four Components

The buying state is a specific emotional configuration. All four elements need to be present before the pitch lands the way it should.

Component What It Means Priming Question That Activates It
Pain Vivid They're feeling the cost of the current situation, not just describing it "How long have you been dealing with this? What's that actually cost you over that time?"
Future Vivid They can see and feel the desired future state in present tense "Describe what your career looks like 12 months from now if this is completely handled."
Gap Acknowledged They see clearly that they can't get to the future from here without something changing "What's been the main thing that's kept you from closing that gap yourself?"
Open to Solution They're ready to hear what can bridge the gap, not skeptical or defended "If there was a way to compress that 3-year timeline into 90 days, what would that be worth to you?"

The Worst Priming Question

"Are you ready to get started?"

This is the worst closing/priming question in sales and it gets asked constantly. Here's why it fails: it puts the prospect in an evaluation mode. They now have to consciously decide if they're "ready" — which activates System 2, creates resistance, and invites objections. It also signals that the rep wants the sale, which collapses the frame.

What to use instead: Any question that invites them to see themselves already in the future. "What would your first 30 days look like if you were in this program?" gets them thinking from inside the decision — not evaluating it from outside.

Priming Question Sequences

Sequence 1 — Pain Activation

"How long has this been going on?" → "What have you tried that hasn't worked?" → "What's it cost you to stay in this spot — in income, in confidence, in time?" → "At this pace, where do you see yourself in 12 months?"

This sequence takes someone from mild dissatisfaction to acute awareness of the cost of staying put. Use it when the prospect isn't fully feeling the pain.

Sequence 2 — Future Activation

"What does your ideal version of this career look like?" → "What would you need to be doing differently to get there?" → "If you were completely on track 6 months from now, what would that look like specifically?" → "What's the number — in income, per month — that would feel like you've broken through?"

This sequence activates aspiration and specificity. Use it when the prospect is emotionally flat or hasn't articulated what they actually want.

Sequence 3 — Pre-Close Priming

"If everything about this program is exactly what you said you needed, what would be the reason not to start today?" → "What would have to be true for this to be the right move?" → "Is there anything that would need to happen before you could move forward?"

This sequence surfaces and pre-empts objections before the close. It also primes the prospect to articulate the conditions under which they'd say yes — which often means they're already thinking about what yes looks like.

Call Exchange — Priming Sequence in Discovery

Priming Pain Vivid + Future Vivid Before the Pitch
Rep
"How long have you been in this spot — where you feel like you're capable of more but it's not showing up in the numbers?"
Primes duration of pain. Makes the cost feel cumulative.
Prospect
"Honestly about a year and a half. Maybe two years."
Rep
"Two years. And in those two years, what have you tried to change it?"
Primes the history of failed attempts. Makes the prospect feel the accumulated effort without result.
Prospect
"I've watched a lot of YouTube content, tried different scripts, changed companies twice."
Rep
"None of that moved it. So let me ask you the other side — what does your career look like two years from now if this actually gets fixed?"
Pivots from pain to future. Prospect now speaks from their best self. Pain and aspiration are both vivid — ideal state for the pitch.
Prospect
"I want to be making $25-30K a month. I want to be known as someone who can close anything. I want to have options."
The buying state is now active. Pain acknowledged. Future vivid. The next thing the rep says will land on warm ground.

Priming Questions Checklist

  • ✓
    Activate pain with duration. "How long" makes the cost cumulative. It turns a problem into a pattern.
  • ✓
    Ask about failed attempts before you pitch solutions. It establishes that the problem isn't simple, and that what they've tried hasn't worked. Your solution is framed as different.
  • ✓
    Pivot to future before the pitch. Get them speaking from their ambitious self before they evaluate your offer. Aspirational state = open, not defended.
  • ✗
    Don't prime with closed questions. "Are you happy with your current numbers?" gets a yes/no and closes the state rather than opening it.
  • ✗
    Don't rush past the pain. If the prospect says "yeah I'm not hitting my numbers," most reps move on. The elite rep stays: "Tell me more about that. What does that actually feel like on a week-to-week basis?"
Lesson 6 · Section 7

Cognitive Dissonance

The identity-reality gap is the most powerful buying driver in existence. When what someone believes about themselves doesn't match what their results show, the discomfort is unbearable — and they'll move to resolve it.

The Psychology

Leon Festinger — 1957

Cognitive dissonance is what happens when a person holds two contradictory beliefs simultaneously. The internal conflict creates psychological discomfort — and humans are strongly motivated to eliminate that discomfort by changing one of the beliefs or changing a behavior that resolves the contradiction.

This isn't a minor preference. It's a fundamental human drive. Inconsistency in our belief systems feels deeply wrong at a neurological level. We will go to remarkable lengths to restore consistency.

In sales, the most powerful version of cognitive dissonance is the identity-reality gap: the prospect believes they are capable of more than their current results show. That gap — between who they believe they are and what their life looks like right now — is not a comfortable place to sit. Buying is one way to resolve it.

The Identity-Reality Gap

How It Works

The prospect carries two beliefs simultaneously. Belief 1: "I am someone with the capability to earn $30,000 a month, to be excellent at what I do, to build a career I'm proud of." Belief 2: "My results this month are $9,000, I lost three deals I should have won, and I don't know what I'm doing wrong."

Those two beliefs cannot comfortably coexist. The dissonance between them creates pressure. That pressure is what drives people to look for solutions — and it's why your ideal prospect is someone who already believes they're capable of more. The gap is already there. You're not creating it.

Three Ways People Resolve Dissonance

Lower the identity belief: "Maybe I'm not actually that good. Maybe this is just where I cap out." This is the internal narrative that kills ambition. Your prospect has probably been flirting with this one.

Ignore the reality: "I'm just in a bad streak. It'll turn around." This is denial. Temporary. The dissonance keeps building until it can't be ignored.

Act to close the gap: Find the training, the coach, the program that aligns their results with their belief in themselves. This is the buying decision. This is what you offer.

Elicitation — Surface the Gap Without Creating It

The ethical line matters here. The goal is to surface the dissonance that already exists in the prospect — not to manufacture it artificially by making them feel inadequate. There's a real difference between helping someone see their own gap clearly and creating a gap by making them feel bad about themselves. One is useful. The other is manipulation.

The Elicitation Questions

These questions surface the identity belief first, then bring in the reality. The gap appears on its own — you don't insert it.

Step Question What It Does
1. Identity "Deep down, what do you believe you're actually capable of in this career?" Gets them to state their identity belief out loud. Now it's real.
2. Reality "And where are you actually right now — what do the numbers look like?" Brings in the reality. The gap is now visible to both of you.
3. Gap "So there's a gap between what you believe about yourself and what's showing up. How long has that been true?" Names the dissonance explicitly. Prospect now feels the pressure to resolve it.
4. Openness "What would have to change for those two things to match?" Opens them to the solution. They're now internally motivated to act.

Why This Produces Better Clients

Internally Motivated vs. Externally Pushed

When a prospect buys because they genuinely feel the identity-reality gap and want to close it, they show up differently than someone who was pushed into a decision. They do the work. They engage with the program. They get results. They refer people.

When a prospect is pressured or manipulated into a decision, they often experience buyer's remorse, disengage early, or become the difficult student who blames the program when things are hard. The best buyers are always internally motivated buyers.

"We find out where you suck and make you good."

— JJ Hernandez

Call Exchange — Surfacing the Identity-Reality Gap

Cognitive Dissonance Elicitation in Discovery
Rep
"I want to ask you something and I want you to answer honestly, not the modest version — what do you actually believe you're capable of in this career? Not what you're doing right now. What you believe is possible for you."
Gets the identity belief on the table. The "not the modest version" permission removes the instinct to undersell themselves.
Prospect
"I genuinely believe I could be making $25-30K a month. I know I have the personality for it. I know I can connect with people. I just haven't figured out the technical side yet."
Rep
"And right now you're at what — $8K, $9K? How long has there been that gap between what you believe about yourself and what your numbers actually show?"
Brings in the reality. Names the gap. Makes the dissonance explicit without judgment.
Prospect
"Honestly, about 18 months. And it's starting to wear on me."
"It's starting to wear on me" — they feel the dissonance acutely. The pressure to resolve it is real. The rep doesn't need to create it. It's already there.
Rep
"That's the gap we close. Let me show you what that actually looks like."
Simple. Direct. The pitch that follows lands on a prospect who is internally motivated to close their own identity-reality gap.
Lesson 7 · Section 7

Identity Consistency

People act in ways consistent with what they've said about themselves. Elicit the statement in discovery. Use it at the close. The most powerful close you'll ever run is the prospect's own words.

The Consistency Principle

Cialdini — Influence

One of Cialdini's six principles of influence is commitment and consistency. Once people make a statement — especially a public one about who they are — they feel internal and social pressure to act in ways that are consistent with that statement. Inconsistency with our own stated identity is psychologically uncomfortable. We move to resolve it.

This is different from cognitive dissonance in an important way. Cognitive dissonance is about the gap between identity and results. Identity consistency is about the gap between what you've stated about yourself and the action you're considering. When you elicit an identity statement and then show how an action is consistent with it, you remove the internal friction that creates hesitation.

The close that uses the prospect's own words is the most natural close in sales. You're not convincing them of anything — you're simply pointing out that the action they're considering is exactly what the person they said they are would do.

Eliciting Identity Statements in Discovery

The Questions That Produce Identity Statements

These questions invite the prospect to describe themselves in terms of values, identity, and self-concept — not just goals or results. The answers become the material you'll use at the close.

Question Sample Identity Statement It Produces
"What do you believe about your potential in this career?" "I genuinely believe I have what it takes — I just haven't had the right guidance."
"What kind of person do you want to be professionally?" "Someone who takes their development seriously and doesn't make excuses."
"When you've made big decisions in the past, what do you think drives them?" "I invest in myself. When I see something that will genuinely help me, I don't hesitate."
"What separates the people in your field who reach the top from those who don't?" "The ones who get there don't wait around for things to change on their own. They make moves."

Write it down. When a prospect makes a strong identity statement in discovery, remember the exact phrasing. Paraphrase it back at the close using their words — not your interpretation of their words. The closer the language, the more powerful the consistency pull.

The Commitment Bridge at the Close

How to Use the Statement

At the close, you bridge directly from what they said about themselves to the action in front of them. The structure is simple: remind them of their own statement, then show how the decision is a direct expression of it.

01
Step 1
Recall the Statement
Bring their exact words back. "You mentioned earlier that you're someone who invests in yourself when you see something that's genuinely going to help you."
02
Step 2
Connect It to the Decision
"This is that decision. This is the one that closes the gap between where you are and where you said you know you can be."
03
Step 3
Silence
Don't add anything else. The prospect is now holding a choice between saying yes (consistent with their stated identity) or saying no (inconsistent with who they said they are). That internal pressure is very real. Let it work.

Call Exchange — Identity Consistency at the Close

Elicitation in Discovery + Close Using Their Own Words
Rep (discovery)
"What kind of professional do you want to be — not just in terms of income, but in terms of how you show up and how serious you are about your own development?"
Prospect
"I want to be someone who doesn't cut corners. Someone who actually does the work and invests in themselves. I'm tired of doing things halfway."
Identity statement locked in. Rep notes the exact phrasing: "invests in themselves," "doesn't cut corners," "tired of doing things halfway."
Rep (30 minutes later, at close)
"Earlier you said something that stuck with me. You said you're tired of doing things halfway. That you want to be someone who actually does the work and invests in themselves. This is that moment. Saying yes to this is the most consistent thing you could do with who you said you are. Saying no is going halfway — again."
Their own words. No new pressure. Just a mirror. Saying no now means contradicting their own stated identity. The internal pressure to be consistent is doing the work.
Prospect
"Yeah... you're right. Let's do it."
Lesson 8 · Section 7

Future Self Projection

Talking about the future creates distance. Speaking from the future creates urgency. Get the prospect to describe their life as if it already happened — and watch everything change.

About the Future vs. From the Future

The Core Distinction

When most reps ask about the future, they use future tense: "What would your life look like if you hit $30K a month?" The prospect answers from a distance. It's hypothetical. It's abstract. It feels like a fantasy, not a plan. The emotional activation is low.

The shift is subtle but critical: you ask them to describe the future as if they're already living it, in present tense. "You're six months in. You're making $30K a month consistently. Describe what a Tuesday looks like for you." Now the brain has to actually simulate the experience — and simulation activates the same neural pathways as reality. The emotional activation is high. The future feels real.

Present-tense future descriptions create felt urgency — not because you're applying pressure, but because the future self becomes real enough to want. Once it's real, not getting there starts to feel like a loss.

Questions That Activate Future Self

Talking ABOUT the Future Speaking FROM the Future
"What would your career look like if this worked out?" "It's 12 months from now. Everything's gone right. Describe what your career looks like right now."
"How much would you want to be earning?" "You're making $30K a month consistently. What does a week feel like for you?"
"What goals are you working toward?" "You've hit every goal you mentioned. What are you proud of right now?"
"What's your dream income?" "The income is handled. That's not even the stress it used to be. What does that free you up to focus on?"

Watch for present tense in their answer. When a prospect slips from "I would want..." to "I'm doing..." or "I feel..." — they've mentally stepped into the future self. That's the moment. The buying desire is at its highest point right then.

The Moment When It Happens — and What to Do

Reading the Shift

You'll hear it in the language. The prospect starts answering in future tense ("I would want to be") and then something shifts — they start using present tense ("I'm doing two calls a day, I'm picking my clients, I don't have to worry about money anymore"). Their pace might slow. Their voice might change. They're no longer describing a hypothetical — they're simulating an experience.

This is the most important moment on a call. Do not interrupt it. Let them stay there. Let them fully inhabit the future state. The more time they spend there, the more real it becomes, and the more returning to the current reality feels like a step backward.

What to Say at That Moment

When you sense they're fully in the future state, a soft bridge: "That's the version of your career this program is built to produce. Let's talk about what it takes to get there."

Or, at the close: "That version of your career you just described — that's what's on the other side of this decision."

Don't over-explain. The prospect's future self just made the case. You're just pointing at it.

Why This Creates Genuine Urgency

The Mechanism

Once the future self feels real, every day between now and that version of their career feels like time lost. The urgency doesn't come from "limited spots" or "price going up" — it comes from the prospect's own desire to get to the place they just vividly experienced. That's the most durable urgency there is because you didn't manufacture it. They generated it themselves.

Call Exchange — Future Self Activation

Getting the Prospect to Speak AS Their Future Self
Rep
"Let me ask you something. It's 12 months from now. Everything has gone the way you wanted it to. You're not stressed about money anymore. Describe what a Tuesday looks like for you — not in theory, in practice."
Present tense setup. Specific day. "Not in theory, in practice" pushes them to actually simulate rather than abstractly describe.
Prospect
"I wake up without checking my phone first thing. I've got two or three calls lined up that I've already pre-screened. I'm not chasing leads — they're coming to me because of my reputation. I'm making maybe $25K that month, which means I can actually pay off some things, maybe take a trip with my family. I'm closing well because I understand what I'm doing, not just winging it."
Present tense throughout. "I wake up," "I've got," "I'm making," "I'm closing." They're in the future self now. This is the moment. Do not interrupt.
Rep
[pause] "That's the version of your career this program is built around. Let's talk about what it takes to get there."
Simple. Doesn't over-sell it. The future self the prospect just described is doing the persuading. The rep just pointed at it.
Lesson 9 · Section 7

Loss Aversion

Kahneman's research is clear: losses feel roughly 2.5x more painful than equivalent gains feel good. Selling what someone will gain is half as powerful as helping them see what they're already losing.

The Research

Kahneman and Tversky — Prospect Theory

In their landmark work on Prospect Theory, Kahneman and Tversky demonstrated something that overturns how most salespeople think about value. People do not evaluate gains and losses symmetrically. Losing $100 feels significantly worse than gaining $100 feels good. The pain of loss is roughly 2 to 2.5 times more powerful than the pleasure of equivalent gain.

This asymmetry has profound implications for how you frame benefits, how you talk about the cost of inaction, and what motivates people to actually make decisions rather than stay comfortable where they are.
