[Pause] "I hear you. I want to be honest with you — if this isn't the right move for you right now, I'd genuinely rather you not join. This only works when someone is fully in. What specifically are you working through?"
Detachment Close. All five frames still present as unified posture beneath it. Rep is simultaneously authoritative, managing time, evaluating, representing something selective, and genuinely detached from the outcome. This is frame integration.

Internalized vs. Memorized — The Real Difference

  • ✓
    Internalized frame shows up on bad calls, not just good ones. When the prospect is hostile, when nothing is going right, when you're in your head — the internalized frame is still there. The memorized frame disappears under those conditions. That's the test.
  • ✓
    Internalized frame doesn't require effort to maintain. The rep who knows the frames is working to hold them. You can see it if you watch closely — a slight effort quality. The rep who has internalized them is just being. No effort. The frame is their natural operating state.
  • ✓
    Internalized frame makes you more present, not less. When you're not thinking about which frame to run, your full attention is on the prospect. You hear things you would have missed. You ask questions you would have skipped over. The conversation gets better. The close rate goes up not because your technique improved but because your presence improved.

The Final Frame

The Frame That Contains All the Others

Beneath Power, Time, Analyst, Prize, and Detachment — there is one frame that makes all of them possible. It doesn't have a name in Klaff's framework because it's not a tactic. It's a state.

Genuine certainty in service. You know this program changes lives. You've seen it. You believe in it. And you're on this call not to take money from a prospect but to help a specific human being make the decision that's actually right for them. That orientation — certainty plus genuine care for the other person — is what makes every other frame real instead of performed.

When you have that, the five frames aren't strategies anymore. They're just how you naturally show up. Authority comes naturally when you're certain. Detachment comes naturally when you genuinely care more about their outcome than your commission. The Prize Frame is natural when you believe the community you're protecting is worth protecting.

"The strongest frame always wins. And the strongest frame you can run is genuine certainty that you're here to help someone change their life — not to close a deal."

— JJ Hernandez, Sales.io

Section 9 Complete.

You've covered what a frame is, the five frames and when to run each one, how to identify and replace your default frame, how to handle frame collision, the Detachment Close, why you'd rather lose the sale than lose the frame, certainty transfer, the Frame Attack Drill, and what integration looks like in practice.

The work now is reps. Every call is a repetition. Review every recording. Run the drill. Notice where your frame shifts and return to center faster each time. There is no ceiling on how strong a frame can get. The closers who operate at the highest level are just further along on the same path you're walking right now.

S10
The Setting System
Lesson 1 · Section 10 · Coach: Jalen Hoxie

The Setter's Role in the Sales Architecture

The set is won or lost before the closer ever picks up the phone. Understanding why is the foundation of everything else in this section.

What a Setter Actually Does

The Real Job Description

Most setters think their job is to book calls. It's not. Booking calls is the output of the job. The actual job is to identify the right person, create genuine interest, qualify their fit, and prime them for a productive conversation with the closer.

If all you're doing is booking calls, you're a scheduling bot. That's not what this role is. The setter is the first line of intelligence in the sales process. What you learn and how you show up determines whether the closer walks into a warm conversation or a cold interrogation.

The setter's three functions, in order of importance:

1. Filter — Keep unqualified people off the closer's calendar.

2. Prime — Create genuine curiosity and forward momentum before the call begins.

3. Book — Get the right person onto the calendar at a time they'll actually show.

Why Most Setters Get This Backwards

Most setters focus entirely on #3 because that's what gets tracked on the surface. Booking rate goes up, everyone's happy — until show rate tanks, close rate collapses, and the closer's burning out on calls that go nowhere.

Volume without filter is just noise. You are not being paid to fill a calendar. You are being paid to generate real revenue opportunities. That distinction changes how you approach every interaction.

Why a Bad Set Is Worse Than No Set

An unqualified call does more damage than a blank calendar slot.

When a closer spends 45 minutes with someone who was never a real prospect, they lose the time, the energy, and the mental state for the next call. A string of bad sets erodes the closer's confidence, tanks their close rate, and eventually creates resentment toward the setter role itself.

One bad set can poison two good ones. That's the real cost you're not tracking.

What Gets Damaged Bad Set Empty Slot
Closer's time 45 minutes wasted, energy drained 45 minutes available for outreach or recovery
Closer's mental state Demoralized, frustrated, distracted going into next call Neutral baseline — can reset intentionally
Revenue impact $0 from bad call + reduced close rate on calls after $0 from empty slot only
Team morale Closer loses trust in setter pipeline No trust damage — just opportunity cost

The First Impression Problem

You Are the Company

The prospect's first real interaction with Sales.io is almost always with the setter. Before they ever hear JJ's voice, before they see a testimonial, before they talk to a closer — they talk to you.

How you show up in that first message, that first call, that first DM exchange is the company's first impression. If you sound like a bot, the company sounds like a faceless machine. If you're pushy, the company sounds desperate. If you're sharp, curious, and composed — that's what the company is.

The halo effect is real. A prospect who had a great experience with the setter walks into the closer's call already warm. They're already predisposed to like what they hear because they already like how this has felt.

A prospect who felt herded onto the calendar walks in skeptical. The closer has to overcome the friction you created before they can even begin the sales process.

What kills the first impression:

Generic openers. Copy-paste DMs. Pitching before connecting. Rushing to book before the prospect is actually interested. Any of these signals "sales machine" — and the prospect's guard goes up immediately.

What a Great Set Delivers to the Closer

  • ✓
    The right person. Budget capacity confirmed. Decision-making authority confirmed. Genuine interest confirmed. Not just willing to show up — actually wanting to.
  • ✓
    Context that matters. The closer knows the prospect's current situation, what they've tried, what's not working, and what they actually want. Not assumptions — information the setter gathered.
  • ✓
    The right mindset. The prospect is curious and open, not defensive. They know what the call is about, what to expect, and why it's worth their time. No confusion, no surprise.
  • ✓
    A clean handoff note. Name, situation summary, what was said, qualification level, and the tone of the conversation documented in Close CRM before the call starts.
  • ✗
    What a bad set delivers instead: A stranger who vaguely agreed to a call, zero context, no priming, no qualification — and a closer who has to start from zero with someone who might not even be a fit.

Exchange — Bad Set vs. Great Set

VERSION 1 — The Setter Who Just Books Calls
Setter
"Hey! I saw your profile and wanted to reach out. We help sales reps hit $20K+ months. Would you be open to a quick call this week?"
Generic. No curiosity about the prospect. Pitching in message 1. No qualification. No priming. Just trying to get a yes on the calendar.
Prospect
"Sure, I guess. When works?"
Setter
"Great! I'll send you a link to book. Looking forward to it!"
Call booked. Zero qualification. Zero context. Closer gets a stranger on the phone and has no idea who they are, what they need, or if they can even afford the program.
VERSION 2 — The Setter Who Delivers a Real Opportunity
Setter
"Hey Marcus — noticed you're doing remote sales and posted about your close rate recently. Curious what offer you're on right now."
Specific observation. Genuine curiosity. No pitch. Opens a real conversation.
Prospect
"I'm closing for a coaching company. Been doing it about 8 months. Close rate's around 18% which feels like it could be better."
Setter
"18% is solid but you're right — with the right framework, reps at your stage are getting to 30%+ pretty consistently. What do you think is holding your rate back?"
Validates without patronizing. Anchors to possibility. Gets the prospect thinking and revealing their pain.
Prospect
"Honestly I think it's my follow-up and how I handle objections. I kind of freeze up when they go sideways."
Setter
"That's exactly the thing we work on — not with scripts but with the actual psychology behind why objections come up. I think a conversation with our head coach would be worth your time. Are you in a place where investing in your skills is on the table right now?"
Qualifies before booking. The prospect now knows what the call is about, has already articulated their pain, and has indicated readiness to invest. The closer walks into a warm room.
Lesson 2 · Section 10 · Coach: Jalen Hoxie

LinkedIn DM Setting

From the first message to a booked call. The sequence, the principles, and what kills your reply rate before the prospect ever types a word back.

The LinkedIn DM Framework

There are four stages to every LinkedIn DM sequence: First Message → Hook → Qualify → Book.

Most setters collapse these into one message. That's why they get ignored. Each stage has one job. When you try to do all four in a single DM, you get a wall of text that reads like a sales email from 2014.

Patience in the sequence is what separates setters who book calls from setters who get left on read.

Why LinkedIn Is Different from Cold Calling

On the phone, you have 10 seconds before they hang up. In a DM, you have one opening line before they close the thread. The medium rewards subtlety. The harder you push in a DM, the faster they ghost you.

LinkedIn is a professional network where people expect to be pitched — so they're already primed to reject anything that smells like a pitch. Your job is to be the thing that doesn't smell like a pitch.

Message 1 Rule — No Pitching

Never pitch in message 1. Not your program. Not your results. Not a "quick call." Not even a hint of what you're selling. The moment a prospect reads a first message and thinks "this is a sales DM," your reply rate drops to near zero.

Message 1 has one job: open a real conversation. That's it. Not book a call. Not qualify. Not sell anything. Start a conversation that a human being would actually want to have.

Dead on Arrival Opens a Conversation
"Hey! I help sales reps hit $20K months. Would you be open to a quick chat?" "Hey Marcus — saw your post about handling price objections. What offer are you closing on right now?"
"Hi [Name], I noticed you're in sales. We have a program that could help you level up your income. Interested?" "Saw you're 8 months into remote closing — how's the offer treating you? Curious what niche you landed in."
"I'd love to jump on a quick 15-minute call to share what we've been doing for reps in your position." "Your close rate comment caught my eye — what do you think is the ceiling on the current offer you're on?"

The Hook — Genuine Curiosity

What Makes a Hook Work

A hook is not a compliment. "Love your content" is not a hook — it's noise. A hook is a specific, genuine observation about something the prospect said, posted, or is experiencing that opens a real question.

The hook creates a reason for the prospect to respond. Not because you're selling something, but because you're engaging with something they actually care about.

01
Hook Type
Profile-Based Hook
Reference something specific on their profile — current role, company type, time in the industry, or a milestone they listed.
"Noticed you've been in remote closing for just under a year — are you still on your first offer or have you made a switch?"
02
Hook Type
Content-Based Hook
Reference a post, comment, or article they shared. Shows you actually looked. Prospects can tell the difference between "I saw your post" and actually reading it.
"Saw your post about the 'I need to think about it' objection — that one trips up most reps. What's been your go-to response to it?"
03
Hook Type
Situation-Based Hook
Reference something about the situation they're likely in based on what you know about their type of role, niche, or stage of career.
"Closing in the coaching niche is a different beast than most remote sales gigs — how are you finding the quality of leads on your end?"

The Qualify-to-Book Bridge

Moving from Conversation to Calendar

Once the prospect has engaged and revealed something about their situation, you have context to work with. Now you qualify — but it cannot feel like a form. It has to feel like the natural next step of a conversation someone is already enjoying.

The bridge question does two things at once: it qualifies their readiness to invest and it transitions toward a call, without making it feel like that's what just happened.

The bridge formula: Acknowledge what they said → add a specific insight → ask if investing in a solution is on the table right now.

Example: "That's actually the exact thing we dig into — the psychology behind why prospects stall and how to move them without pressure. Are you in a place where investing in that skill set is something you'd consider right now?"

If they say yes — or even "depends on what it costs" — you have a qualified prospect. Book the call. If they say no, don't force it. Let the conversation breathe and follow up later.

The premature book attempt. Asking "want to jump on a call?" before the prospect has revealed their pain, confirmed interest, and indicated budget readiness is the most common DM mistake. You get a reluctant yes that turns into a no-show.

Book when they're warm. Not when you've hit your outreach quota for the day.

Full DM Sequence — Start to Booked Call

FULL DM SEQUENCE — LinkedIn Cold Outreach to Booked Call
Setter — Message 1 (Day 1)
"Hey Taylor — saw you're about a year into remote closing. Are you still on your first offer or have you made a switch since you started?"
Specific to them. No pitch. Opens a real question they'll actually want to answer.
Prospect — Reply
"Still on my first one — coaching niche. It's decent but I feel like I've hit a ceiling around 18-20% close rate."
Setter — Message 2
"18-20% is solid for where you are — but you're right that there's more there. Most reps plateau around that mark because they're still relying on tactical moves instead of understanding why people actually buy. What do you think is the bottleneck for you specifically?"
Validates. Introduces a real concept without selling. Invites them to self-diagnose — which creates engagement.
Prospect
"Honestly I think it's how I handle objections. I get kind of defensive and I feel like they sense it."
Setter — Message 3 (Bridge)
"That self-awareness is actually rare — most reps don't know what's breaking down until they see it on a call recording. That defensiveness comes from identity stuff more than technique. It's what we spend real time on. Is investing in that kind of growth something you're looking at right now, or is timing off?"
Qualifies readiness to invest. Natural. Not "wanna jump on a call?" — positions the investment as a real conversation first.
Prospect
"Yeah I'm open to it. Depends what it looks like."
Setter — Message 4 (Book)
"Makes sense. Best way to get clarity on that is a call with our head coach — it's not a pitch session, it's a real conversation about where you are and what getting to 30%+ would actually take. I can get you on for Wednesday or Thursday this week. Which works better?"
Books with a binary choice (Wednesday or Thursday). Frames the call as valuable, not as a sales meeting. Prospect showed up warm, curious, and self-aware.
Lesson 3 · Section 10 · Coach: Jalen Hoxie

Cold Calling Fundamentals

Pattern interrupt openers. The first 10 seconds determine everything. Most calls are lost before the setter ever says what they're calling about.

The Pattern Interrupt

What the Prospect Is Doing Before You Call

Before you dial, the prospect is already bracing. Not because they know it's you — because they've gotten enough sales calls to have a Pavlovian response to unknown numbers. They pick up ready to get off the phone.

Their brain has a script for this: unknown number, probably sales, find a reason to end it fast. Your opener either triggers that script or breaks it. There is no middle ground in the first 10 seconds.

A pattern interrupt is anything that breaks the prospect out of their pre-programmed "sales call" response. Not by being weird or aggressive — by being unexpected in a calm, unhurried way that doesn't match what they were prepared to reject.

The pattern they expect: fast-talking, enthusiastic, immediately pitching a product. The interrupt: slow, certain, curious — and not immediately explaining why you called.

The First 10 Seconds

The prospect decides within 10 seconds whether they're going to engage or tolerate you. Tolerated calls end the moment they find a polite exit. Engaged calls go somewhere.

The single thing that determines engagement in those 10 seconds is whether the opener matches the pattern they expected or breaks it. If it matches, they're already planning their exit. If it breaks it, they're present — even if only for a moment.

What Happens in the Prospect's Head

Second 1-2: Unknown number, already suspicious. Second 3-4: You say your name, they start preparing their out. Second 5-7: You say something that either confirms "sales call" or doesn't match what they expected. Second 8-10: They've made their decision — engage or escape.

The window is real. Don't waste it with enthusiasm, over-introduction, or immediate pitching.

The Cold Call Framework

01
Step 1
Interrupt
Say their name once, calmly. State your name — nothing else. A two-beat pause. Not an excited greeting. Not "how are you today?" Not a company name. Just yours. Let the silence do work.
"Hey Marcus." [pause] "This is Jalen."
02
Step 2
Hook
One specific line that creates just enough intrigue to keep them on the phone for 30 more seconds. Reference their world — their niche, their type of role, their likely situation. Not a pitch. A curiosity trigger.
"I work with remote closers in the coaching space — specifically with reps who've hit a plateau on their close rate and can't figure out why."
03
Step 3
Qualify
Ask one qualifying question. Not a survey — one question that tells you if they're worth continuing the conversation with. Keep your tone low and curious, not interrogating.
"Quick question — are you currently on an offer you're happy with, or is performance the thing you're looking to fix right now?"
04
Step 4
Book
Once they've engaged and given you signal, move to the book. Give two options — not an open-ended "when works for you?" Binary choices reduce friction and move things forward.
"I'd want to get you on a call with our head coach — not a pitch, just a real conversation. Tuesday afternoon or Thursday morning work for you?"

Strong Openers vs. Weak Openers

Weak Opener Strong Opener Why
"Hi [Name]! How are you doing today?" "Hey [Name]." [pause] "This is Jalen." "How are you today" is the universal signal for an incoming sales call. They've heard it 200 times.
"I'm calling from Sales.io and I wanted to share an exciting opportunity..." "I work with remote closers who are stuck below 20% and can't figure out why." Company name + "exciting opportunity" confirms the script they were already prepared to reject.
"Is this a good time to talk?" "Quick question for you — are you still actively closing, or did you step back from it?" "Is this a good time?" hands them the exit ramp. A direct question pulls them into the conversation instead.
"I know you're probably busy but I just wanted to take 2 minutes of your time..." "I'll be quick — I work with closers in your niche and I'm curious about one thing." Leading with apology signals low confidence and frames you as an interruption. Confident curiosity frames you as worth listening to.

Call Exchange — Pattern Interrupt in Practice

VERSION 1 — The Call That Follows the Script They Expected
Setter
"Hi Taylor! How are you doing today? My name is Jalen and I'm calling from Sales.io. We help sales reps like yourself hit their income goals and I was wondering if you'd have a few minutes to talk about that?"
Every word confirms the pattern: enthusiastic tone, company name, vague pitch, asks for time. Prospect's plan-to-exit is confirmed and active.
Prospect
"Um — I'm actually kind of busy right now. Maybe send me an email?"
Classic polite exit. They weren't actually busy. They just knew this call wasn't for them within 6 seconds.
VERSION 2 — The Call That Breaks the Pattern
Setter
"Hey Taylor." [beat] "This is Jalen."
Slow. Name only. No enthusiasm explosion. No company. No pitch setup. The prospect's pattern recognition hasn't fired yet.
Prospect
"...Hey. Who's this?"
Setter
"Jalen — I work with remote closers, specifically reps closing in the coaching or consulting space. Quick question for you — are you still actively on an offer or have you stepped away from it?"
Still no pitch. Just context + one qualifying question. Feels like a real person with a real purpose, not a sales machine running a script.
Prospect
"Yeah I'm still on one. Been doing it about a year."
They're answering. That's the interrupt working. They engaged instead of exiting.
Setter
"Good to know. What's the offer and how's the close rate sitting for you right now?"
Deepens the qualify. Stays calm. No pivot to the pitch yet. Just listening and gathering. The closer is going to get a real conversation because of this opening.
Lesson 4 · Section 10 · Coach: Jalen Hoxie

Tonality in Setting

The setter sets the authority tone for the entire company. Before the prospect ever meets the closer, they've already decided how much trust to extend — based on you.

You Set the Authority Tone

The prospect's perception of Sales.io is built in the first conversation they have with anyone from the team. That's almost always the setter. How you sound determines how the company sounds.

If you sound uncertain, the company sounds uncertain. If you sound like you're reading a script, the company sounds mechanical. If you sound calm, certain, and knowledgeable — the company earns trust before the closer even introduces themselves.

The closer inherits your tonality. A warm, authoritative set makes the closer's opening easy. A weak or pushy set makes the closer climb a hill before the call even starts.

What "Authority Tone" Actually Means

It's not aggression. It's not loudness. Authority tone is calm certainty — the vocal quality of someone who knows what they're talking about and doesn't need your approval to continue. It reads as competence, and competence transfers trust.

The prospect doesn't have to understand why they trust you. They just feel it in the pace, the depth, and the consistency of your voice.

Tonality Principles for Setting

01
Principle
Calm Certainty Over Enthusiasm
Enthusiasm signals "I need you to like this." Calm certainty signals "I know this is good — whether you see it yet is up to you." One comes from needing the outcome. The other comes from knowing the product is solid. Only one creates trust.
Wrong: "This is SO exciting — we've helped so many people and I think you'd love it!" Right: "This works. I've watched it work. I'd want to get you on a call so you can see what specifically applies to your situation."
02
Principle
Slower Pace Than You Think You Need
Under pressure, pace increases. Speed signals desperation, nervousness, or that you're trying to get through something before they hang up. Slowing down — even slightly below your natural pace — signals security. It says you're not worried about losing this one.
Practice saying your opener at 80% of your natural speed. It will feel uncomfortably slow. That's usually exactly right.
03
Principle
Downward Inflection on Statements
Upward inflection (where your voice rises at the end of a sentence) turns statements into questions. It signals you need agreement. Downward inflection on statements signals certainty. You're not asking if they agree — you're telling them what's true.
Upward: "We work with a lot of closers in the coaching space?" (sounds uncertain) Downward: "We work with closers in the coaching space." (sounds certain)
04
Principle
Consistent Volume — Never Compensate
When uncertain, setters go quieter (shrinking) or louder (compensating with energy). Neither works. A steady, even volume throughout the conversation signals you're not reacting emotionally to what they say. It's one of the subtlest and most powerful signals of authority.
If you notice yourself getting louder after an objection, that's compensation. Pause. Reset your volume. Respond at the same level you started at.

DM Tonality vs. Cold Call Tonality

The Medium Changes the Signal

On a cold call, tonality is carried by your voice — pace, pitch, volume, inflection. In a DM conversation, tonality is carried by word choice, message length, and response timing. The principles are the same; the expression is different.

Element In DMs On Cold Calls
Pace Short, direct messages. Don't over-explain. Brevity reads as confidence. Slower than natural speed. Pauses between key phrases. Don't rush through your opener.
Enthusiasm No exclamation points. No "Amazing!" or "That's great!" — reads as performative and salesy. No vocal excitement spikes. Even, warm, certain. The kind of voice that sounds like it's done this many times.
Authority signal Specific observations. Asking real questions. Not following up too fast. Patience signals abundance. Downward inflection. Volume consistency. A beat of silence after the prospect finishes speaking before you respond.
Response to objection Don't immediately respond to pushback. A slightly delayed reply signals you thought about it, not that you panicked. Pause before responding. Never match their energy if they push back. Drop your pace further when they resist.

What Kills Tonality in Setting

Rushing through the opener. When you're nervous or quota-focused, you speed up. The prospect hears the speed and knows something's off — even if they can't name it. Slow is authority. Fast is desperation.

Upward inflection on statements. "We work with a lot of reps who are in a similar situation to yours?" — that question mark at the end kills credibility. Train yourself to land your statements. Certainty down. Agreement-seeking up.

Filler words. "Um," "uh," "like," "you know," "basically," "kind of" — these words telegraph that you're filling space because you're uncertain. The prospect reads uncertainty in a setter as uncertainty in the product. Record your calls. Count your fillers. Eliminate them.

Vocal mirroring on resistance. When the prospect pushes back or sounds irritated, matching their energy confirms the confrontation. Dropping your pitch and pace slightly when they push says: I'm not rattled, and I'm not going anywhere. That's authority.

Exchange — Same Words, Different Tonality

VERSION 1 — Setter With Weak Tonality
Setter
"Hi! Is this Marcus? Um — okay great, so I'm calling because, uh, we work with sales reps and I think we might be able to help you? Like, we do a lot of work with closers who want to hit higher income and I was kind of wondering if that's something you'd be interested in talking about?"
Speed. Filler words. Upward inflections. Ends with seeking permission. Every word here signals uncertainty — and the prospect reads it as a signal that the company, the product, and the setter are all uncertain.
Prospect
"I'm not really interested in that kind of thing, thanks."
VERSION 2 — Setter With Authority Tonality
Setter
"Hey Marcus." [pause] "This is Jalen. I work with remote closers — specifically in the coaching and consulting space. One question for you: are you happy with where your close rate is right now, or is that something you're actively working on?"
Calm. Certain. Unhurried. No fillers. Downward inflection on every statement. The question at the end is direct, not permission-seeking. The prospect has no reason to dismiss this as noise — it sounds like a real person who knows what they're talking about.
Prospect
"I mean — it could be better. I'm around 20% right now."
They're answering honestly. That's what authority tonality earns — real responses instead of rehearsed exits.
Setter
"20% is a real floor to work from. Most reps at that stage have a specific leak — usually objection handling or the close sequence itself. Worth a conversation with our head coach to find out which one it is for you."
Specific. Assured. No excitement, no oversell. Just clean, confident information. The closer is going to walk into trust that was built here.
Lesson 5 · Section 10 · Coach: Jalen Hoxie

Qualifying on the Set

What to know before you book, how to find out without it feeling like an interrogation, and what to do when someone is almost — but not quite — a fit.

What to Qualify

Four things must be true for a booked call to have a real chance of converting. Budget capacity. Decision-making authority. Timeline. Genuine interest. Know these before you send the calendar link.

You don't need their exact finances. You don't need a verbal commitment. You need enough signal on each one to know they belong on the closer's calendar.

01
Qualifier
Budget Capacity
Not "do you have $7,500?" — that's too direct and creates defensiveness. Instead: confirm they have income coming in and that investing in their growth is something they'd consider. "Are you actively making money in sales right now, or are you pre-revenue?" and "Is investing in your skill set something you're in a position to do, or is timing off?" are your two budget signals.
"Is investing in that kind of growth something you're in a position to do right now, or is timing off?" — Simple. Non-threatening. Tells you what you need to know.
02
Qualifier
Decision-Making Authority
Will they be the one deciding? Or is there a partner, spouse, or financial gatekeeper who needs to be part of the conversation? You don't need to ask "are you the decision maker" — that sounds like a B2B form. Just ask naturally whether they typically talk major decisions through with someone or handle them independently.
"For something like this — a real investment in yourself — is that a solo decision for you or is there someone else you typically loop in?" Low friction. Surfaces a potential obstacle before the closer hits it.
03
Qualifier
Timeline
Are they looking to make a change now, or is this exploratory? "Now" doesn't mean today — it means within the current quarter. Someone who's "thinking about it" for next year is a follow-up, not a booking. Don't book a closer's time for a prospect who isn't in motion.
"Is this something you're actively working on right now, or is it more of a down-the-road thing?" If they say down the road, ask what needs to change for them to be ready. That tells you what the real objection is.
04
Qualifier
Genuine Interest
Are they engaging with real curiosity, or just being polite? A prospect who is asking follow-up questions, revealing their situation, and showing initiative in the conversation is interested. A prospect who gives one-word answers and is waiting for you to finish is being polite. Don't book the second type.
Genuine interest sounds like: "Yeah I've actually been thinking about this for a while — I just didn't know the right path." Polite tolerance sounds like: "Yeah, sure, that sounds interesting I guess."

How to Qualify Without an Interrogation

The Conversation That Qualifies

Qualification is not a checklist you run through. It happens inside the conversation you're already having. If you've opened well and the prospect is talking, most qualification surfaces naturally — you just have to be listening for it.

When they tell you they've been closing for a year, you know they have income. When they say "my wife and I have been talking about this," you know there's a second decision maker. When they say "I want to make a change this quarter," you know the timeline. You don't have to ask — you have to listen.

The bridge question does two jobs: it qualifies readiness to invest AND transitions naturally toward a call — without making it feel like a form or a gate.

Example: "Is investing in that skill set something you're in a position to do right now, or is timing off?" This confirms budget and urgency at the same time. One question. Two answers.

When to Ask Directly

If the conversation has been going well but you haven't gotten signal on one qualifier, you can ask — but frame it as clarifying, not gatekeeping. "One thing I want to make sure of before I get you connected with our coach" — this positions the question as a service to them, not a filter you're running them through.

The Minimum Qualification Standard

What Must Be True Before You Book

The closer's calendar is not a dumping ground. Every booking should meet this minimum bar before you send the link.

  • ✓
    Active income from sales or a related field. They're not pre-revenue. They have some capacity to invest even if it requires planning.
  • ✓
    Expressed a specific problem. Not "I want to make more money" in the abstract — a specific pain point that connects to what Sales.io solves.
  • ✓
    Open to investing in a solution. Said or implied that they would consider a real investment to solve the problem. Not just "yeah sounds interesting."
  • ✓
    Decision is theirs to make (or partner informed). If there's a spouse or partner, they know a call is happening and aren't blindsided by the investment conversation.
  • ✗
    Do NOT book if: They're pre-revenue and have no near-term income source, they're just "curious" with no urgency, they're in active financial crisis, or they gave you no genuine engagement — just polite tolerance.

When Someone Passes on Some Criteria but Not All

Don't force a booking to hit your numbers. If someone passes on interest and timeline but fails on budget or decision-making authority, you have a follow-up lead — not a call. Booking them anyway wastes the closer's time and usually results in a no-show or an unqualifiable call.

Situation Right Move
Interested + problem identified, but budget is tight right now Book the call. Budget conversation belongs to the closer. Your job is to confirm capacity to invest in principle — not get a commitment on price.
Interested + budget fine, but decision involves a spouse they haven't told Ask them to give their partner a heads-up that the call is happening before you book. A blindsided spouse kills the close.
Interested, but timeline is "next quarter" Don't book now. Ask what needs to happen before they're ready. Follow up at the right time. Patience on this one pays off more than a forced booking.
Engages politely but expresses no real pain or urgency Don't book. Follow up in 2-4 weeks. Forced bookings from people who aren't ready produce no-shows, not closings.

Exchange — Qualifying Without an Interrogation

LIVE QUALIFY — Natural Conversation That Surfaces All Four Criteria
Prospect
"Yeah I've been closing in the health coaching space for about nine months. My close rate is around 16%."
Income: confirmed (active closer). Timeline signal: 9 months in, actively working.
Setter
"16% for 9 months in is honest — there's clearly something breaking down in a specific part of the process. What feels like the weakest link when you replay your calls?"
Doesn't jump to the book. Gets specific pain surfaced. Prospect is now doing the work of identifying their problem.
Prospect
"Honestly it's whenever they say they need to think about it. I just kind of fold and let them go. My manager says I'm leaving 40% of my closes on the table."
Specific pain. Motivated. Problem identified. The closer has a clear angle before the call starts.
Setter
"That's one of the most fixable things in sales — it's not technique, it's identity. Is investing in fixing this something you're in a position to do right now, or is timing off?"
Budget + timeline qualifier in one sentence. Doesn't feel like a form. Follows naturally from what just happened in the conversation.
Prospect
"Yeah — I mean it'd be a real investment but I'm making money. I'd just need to know it's worth it."
Budget: confirmed. Interest: confirmed. That's the booking signal. Now you close for the call — not the program.
Setter
"That's a completely fair position. The call with our head coach will give you exactly that — not a pitch, just a real conversation about what's breaking down and what fixing it would actually look like. Do you make decisions like this solo or do you typically talk it through with someone first?"
Decision authority: confirmed. Then books with a binary choice.
Lesson 6 · Section 10 · Coach: Jalen Hoxie

Setter Objections

"I'm not interested" is not a no. It's a "not yet." The setter who understands this books calls that setters who fold never reach.

"I'm Not Interested" Is Not a No

What the Objection Actually Means

"I'm not interested" in the first 30 seconds of a cold interaction does not mean the person has evaluated what you're offering and decided it's not for them. It means you haven't broken the pattern yet, or you triggered their sales guard by moving too fast.

It's a reflexive response, not a considered decision. The setter who treats it as a final no is not getting rejected — they're folding before the real conversation even started.

The principle that applies here is the same one that applies everywhere in Sales.io: you don't need to fight objections, you need to get curious about them.

When someone says "I'm not interested," they're usually saying one of four things: "I don't understand what you're offering," "I've been burned by this before," "I don't think this applies to me," or "I don't trust you yet." None of those are a closed door. All of them are a conversation.

The Top 5 Setter Objections

Objection Response Approach
"I'm not interested." Validate + get curious. "Fair enough — out of curiosity, is it that the timing's off or that sales training specifically isn't something you're looking at right now?" Surfaces the real reason.
"I already have a coach / program." Don't compete. Get curious. "Got it — how's that going for you so far? What's it doing for your close rate?" If it's working, disengage gracefully. If there are gaps, they'll tell you.
"I don't have the money right now." Don't push. Qualify timeline. "Understood — is the plan to revisit this once you're in a better place financially, or is the growth piece not a priority right now at all?" Separates the broke-but-motivated from the genuinely not interested.
"I'm not looking for anything right now." "Makes sense — are you in a good spot with your close rate or are you still working through things?" Moves the conversation to their reality, not your pitch.
"Send me more info." (Brush-off) Decline the redirect. "I could do that, but honestly it won't give you what the conversation would. What would make a 20-minute call worth your time?" Forces them to either engage or end it clean.

The Pattern — Validate, Curiosity, Reframe

Why This Pattern Works

Validate: you're not arguing with their objection. You're acknowledging it as real, which lowers their defenses because they feel heard. Curiosity: a genuine question moves the conversation from sales script to actual dialogue. Reframe: once they've revealed what the real concern is, you can address it directly — not with a rehearsed answer, but with something that actually fits what they just told you.

01
Step 1
Validate
Don't fight the objection. Acknowledge it without flinching. A setter who immediately pushes against an objection signals that they were expecting agreement and weren't ready for resistance. Validation signals you're unfazed.
"That makes sense." / "Fair enough." / "Understood." — short, genuine, not a set-up line.
02
Step 2
Curiosity Question
Ask a question that reveals what's actually behind the objection. Not a rhetorical question designed to make them feel silly — a real one you'd genuinely want the answer to. The question should feel like the natural next thing a person with no agenda would ask.
"Out of curiosity — is it that the timing's off, or that this kind of investment specifically isn't on your radar right now?"
03
Step 3
Reframe
Use what they told you in step 2. Don't use a pre-scripted response — respond to their actual answer. If they said timing, address timing. If they said trust, address trust. The reframe works because it's specific to them, not generic.
If they said "timing" — "Got it. What would need to change for timing to be right?" If they said "I've tried programs before" — "That's fair. What made those not work for you?"

When to Push and When to Let Go

Not every objection should be worked. The setter's job is to find real opportunities, not convert every contact into a booking. Wasting time on someone who is genuinely not a fit or genuinely not ready hurts your numbers the same way bad bookings do — it consumes time that could have gone to real leads.

Push When:

The objection is reflexive, not considered. They said "not interested" in under 10 seconds. They're engaged but hesitant. The real objection is different from the stated one. They revealed a pain point that connects to what you offer. They have the markers of a qualified prospect.

Let Go When:

They've clarified after two questions and it's genuinely not a fit. They're pre-revenue with no near-term path. They're in financial distress. They've said no twice with specific reasoning, not reflexive deflection. Pushing past this damages the brand and wastes your time.

The graceful exit is also a skill. When you do let go, do it on your terms — not theirs. "Understood — look, if anything changes on your end, I'll leave it open. Take care." Clean, professional, no resentment. They might come back. They definitely won't if you burned it.

Exchange — Setter Who Folds vs. Setter Who Holds

VERSION 1 — Setter Who Folds
Prospect
"I'm not really interested, thanks."
Setter
"Oh okay, no problem! Sorry for bothering you. Have a great day!"
Immediate capitulation. Apologizes for the call. This wasn't a no — it was a reflex. The setter read it as a closed door and exited. Real lead lost.
VERSION 2 — Setter Who Holds
Prospect
"I'm not really interested, thanks."
Setter
"Fair enough." [pause] "Out of curiosity — is it that the timing's off, or that sales training specifically isn't something you're looking at right now?"
Validates without caving. Curiosity question pulls back the curtain on the real objection. Calm and unhurried.
Prospect
"Honestly I've done a few programs and they never really moved the needle. I don't want to waste money again."
Real objection revealed: past failure, not current disinterest. This is a workable conversation.
Setter
"That's fair — and I get why that would make you cautious. What was it about those programs that didn't work? Was it the material, the structure, or just nothing stuck?"
Doesn't defend other programs. Doesn't pivot to the pitch. Gets the specific failure point — which will become the anchor for what makes this different.
Prospect
"It was all theory. No one worked with me on actual calls. I needed someone to watch me and tell me what I was doing wrong."
Setter
"That's the exact gap we fill. Every coach here is actively closing — not teaching from slides. Call review is a core part of the program. I'm not saying book something today, but it'd be worth a conversation to see if this is actually different for you."
Specific reframe using their words. Not overselling. Offers a low-stakes next step. This lead almost walked. Now it's a booking.
Lesson 7 · Section 10 · Coach: Jalen Hoxie

The Booking Confirmation

Reducing no-shows to near zero. The work doesn't stop when they book. Most setters abandon the lead the moment they send the calendar link — that's exactly when the no-show is created.

The No-Show Epidemic

The industry average no-show rate for cold outreach bookings is 40-60%. That means for every 10 calls booked, 4-6 people don't show. That number is not a lead quality problem — it's a confirmation system problem.

Most setters send the calendar link, move to the next lead, and assume the prospect will show. They won't, at that rate. Life gets in the way. The excitement fades. Something comes up. The prospect who was warm yesterday is barely remembering your name today — especially if you haven't followed up since the booking.

Why No-Shows Happen

The excitement of agreeing to a call doesn't sustain itself over 24-72 hours without reinforcement. In that window, the prospect's initial curiosity decays, their daily life takes priority, and unless they've been reminded why the call matters, they'll deprioritize it — or quietly reschedule it to never.

A confirmation sequence doesn't just remind them about the call. It reinforces why the call is worth keeping.

The Confirmation Sequence

01
Touchpoint 1
Immediate Confirmation (Within 5 Minutes of Booking)
Send a message the moment the call is booked. Not just "here's the link" — a warm follow-through that ties back to what they told you in the conversation. Reference their specific pain point. Remind them why they said yes.
"Just sent the link — looking forward to it. Based on what you mentioned about folding on the 'think about it' objection, I think you're going to get a lot of clarity from this call. You're confirmed for [day] at [time]."
02
Touchpoint 2
24-Hour Check-In
The day before the call (or 24 hours out if booked same-week), send a short re-engagement message. Not just "confirming tomorrow" — add something that re-anchors their interest. A relevant question, a quick observation, or a forward-looking framing about what the call will cover.
"Hey Marcus — tomorrow at 2pm, still good for you? One thing to think about before the call: what would your month look like if your close rate went from 18% to 28%? That's what we'll map out."
03
Touchpoint 3
Day-of Reminder (2 Hours Before)
Two hours before the call, a short, warm message. Not a formal reminder — a human one. Keep it brief. Confirm the link is working, remind them of the time and who they're talking to, and give them an easy way to reschedule if something actually came up (better to reschedule than ghost).
"Hey — you're on with Coach Jalen in about 2 hours at 2pm. [link]. If anything came up on your end, let me know and we can move it — otherwise see you there."

What the Confirmation Should Include

  • ✓
    Reference to their specific pain point. Not generic excitement — something tied to what they told you. Shows you were listening and reminds them why they agreed to the call.
  • ✓
    What to expect from the call. Brief and honest. "This is a real conversation about where you are and what the path to [X outcome] looks like — not a pitch session." Manages expectations and reduces pre-call anxiety.
  • ✓
    Who they're talking to. Name of the coach. One sentence of credibility. Makes the call feel more real and personal — not just a generic "discovery call."
  • ✓
    An easy reschedule path. "If anything comes up, just let me know" — this prevents silent no-shows. A prospect who knows they can reschedule without awkwardness is more likely to reschedule than ghost.
  • ✗
    What to leave out: Calendar system auto-emails that feel corporate. Generic "looking forward to it!" without substance. Re-pitching the program. Sending too many messages (more than 3 before the call feels like chasing).

The Mental Vaccine — Priming Against Canceling

The most powerful no-show reducer isn't the reminder. It's priming them against canceling before the idea of canceling even occurs to them.

In the 24-hour message, include something that anchors the cost of not showing up — not a threat, but a real question that makes them feel what they'd be leaving on the table. "What would your month look like if your close rate went from 18% to 28%?" makes the prospect think about the upside. The call becomes an opportunity cost calculation — not showing up means leaving something real on the table.

Why This Works

People don't cancel things they feel invested in. The confirmation sequence builds micro-investment: they replied to your immediate message, they answered your 24-hour question, they feel like there's a real person behind the calendar link — not just an automated booking system. Each touchpoint deepens the commitment slightly.

A prospect who has exchanged 3 messages with you since booking is far less likely to ghost than one who got a single automated calendar invite.

Don't over-confirm. Three touchpoints is the maximum before it becomes annoying. If you're sending five messages before the call, you've crossed from helpful into desperate. The sequence works because it's measured — not because it's frequent.

Exchange — Weak vs. Strong Confirmation

VERSION 1 — The Confirmation That Creates No-Shows
Setter (Immediately After Booking)
"Great! I just sent you the calendar invite. Looking forward to connecting you with our team. See you then!"
Generic. No connection to the conversation. No reinforcement of why the call matters. The prospect will forget this within an hour. No-show risk is high.
Setter (Day Before)
"Just wanted to confirm your call tomorrow at 2pm! Let me know if you have any questions."
Purely logistical. Does nothing to rebuild the momentum from when they booked. The prospect has had 48 hours for the initial curiosity to decay. This doesn't restore it.
VERSION 2 — The Confirmation That Drives Show Rates
Setter (Immediately After Booking)
"Just sent the link — confirmed for Thursday at 2pm. Based on what you mentioned about freezing up when prospects say 'I need to think about it,' that's going to be a big part of what you cover with Jalen. He's been coaching closers through that exact thing for years. You're in good hands."
Specific to their pain. Names the coach. Creates anticipation. Feels like a person wrote it.
Setter (24 Hours Before)
"Hey Marcus — you're on for tomorrow at 2pm. Quick question to think about before the call: if you fixed the 'think about it' leak in your close rate, what would that actually add to your monthly income? Bring that number into the conversation — Jalen will map out whether it's realistic and how long it takes to get there."
Mental vaccine in action. Anchors the opportunity cost. Makes the call feel like something worth protecting, not something easy to skip.
Setter (2 Hours Before)
"Hey — you're up with Jalen in 2 hours. Here's the link: [link]. If anything came up and you need to move it, just let me know. Otherwise see you there."
Brief. Human. No pressure. Gives them the reschedule option so they don't ghost if life happened. Keeps the relationship intact either way.
Lesson 8 · Section 10 · Coach: Jalen Hoxie

Handoff Protocol

The handoff is where most setting-to-closing systems break down. The closer should never walk into a call cold. If they do, the setter didn't finish the job.

Where the System Breaks Down

The single most common failure point in a setter-closer system is the gap between "booked" and "briefed." The setter gets the call on the calendar and moves on. The closer shows up, opens Close CRM, sees a name with no notes, and has to start the call in the dark.

Starting a call in the dark costs 5-10 minutes of rapport-building time that should have been spent on real discovery. In a 45-minute call, that's significant. The closer is re-establishing context that the setter already has. The prospect has to repeat themselves. Nobody wins.

What a Missing Handoff Costs the Closer

The closer's first 60 seconds on a call sets the tone for the entire conversation. If they spend those 60 seconds asking the prospect to re-explain who they are and why they booked, they've started from zero and the prospect feels like they're talking to someone who doesn't know them.

A proper handoff means the closer can open with something specific: "Marcus, I heard you've been closing in the health coaching space for about a year and you're working through the 'I need to think about it' objection — that's actually a really fixable thing." That opener takes 10 seconds and builds instant connection. It's only possible if the setter left a real note.

What the Closer Needs Before Every Call

  • ✓
    Full name and how they found us. LinkedIn outreach, cold call, referral, inbound. Context on how they came in shapes how the closer frames their approach.
  • ✓
    Current situation summary. What do they do, how long have they been doing it, what are the key numbers (close rate, income, offer type, niche). This is what the closer uses to open the call with specificity.
  • ✓
    What was said in the set. The specific pain they revealed. The exact words they used, if memorable. This is the anchor the closer comes back to throughout the call.
  • ✓
    Qualification level. Did they confirm budget capacity? Is there a second decision maker? What's the urgency level? Any objections that came up during the set and how they were handled?
  • ✓
    Energy and tone of the conversation. Were they excited, cautious, skeptical, or just curious? Was there resistance or did it flow easily? The closer's emotional read on the prospect before the call starts directly affects how they open.
  • ✗
    What does NOT count as a handoff: A name in Close with no notes. "He seemed interested." A generic "qualified lead" tag with no context. These give the closer nothing to work with.

The Handoff Format — Close CRM Note

The Standard

Every booked call gets a structured note in Close CRM before the call starts. No exceptions. This is entered by the setter as soon as the call is confirmed — not the day of, not an hour before. As soon as it's booked.

Close CRM Handoff Note — Template
NAME
Marcus Thompson
SOURCE
LinkedIn DM outreach — setter initiated
SITUATION
Remote closer, health coaching niche, 9 months in. Currently at 18% close rate. Has a consistent income (~$5-7K/mo based on conversation). Not pre-revenue.
PAIN POINT (THEIR WORDS)
"I fold whenever they say they need to think about it. My manager says I'm leaving 40% of my closes on the table."
QUALIFICATION
Budget: confirmed willing to invest. Decision maker: solo. Timeline: actively looking now. Past programs: tried one generic program, didn't work because "all theory, no real coaching."
ENERGY / TONE
Self-aware, a little skeptical due to past program experience, but genuinely motivated. Warmed up through the conversation. Came in cautious, left curious.
SETTER NOTES
Address the past program skepticism early — he's worried about getting theory instead of real coaching. Differentiate on the call review and active coaches angle. Don't oversell early.

What a Bad Handoff Does to the First 60 Seconds

A closer who walks in cold has to use the opening of the call to gather information the setter already had. This means the prospect has to repeat themselves, the closer loses the opportunity to open with something personal and specific, and the relationship starts from scratch instead of from where the setter left it.

No Handoff Note Complete Handoff Note
"Hey Marcus, thanks for hopping on. So tell me a bit about yourself and what you're looking to work on." "Marcus — I heard you've been in the health coaching space for about a year, closing around 18%, and that the main thing is what happens when prospects say they need to think about it. That right?"
Prospect spends 5 minutes re-explaining their situation. Feels like a cold call, not a warm referral. Prospect says "yeah exactly." Feels heard before the call even really starts. Trust is already building.
Closer has no angle, no anchor, no specific pain to return to. Call is generic. Closer knows the pain, the skepticism about past programs, and the right angle to differentiate. Call is targeted from the first minute.

Exchange — The Handoff That Changes the First 60 Seconds

SCENARIO — Closer Opening with a Complete Handoff Note
Closer
"Marcus — appreciate you jumping on. I got some context from Jalen before this. You've been closing in the health coaching space for about nine months, running around 18%, and the main thing breaking down is when prospects say they need more time to think — that right?"
Opens with everything from the handoff note. No "tell me about yourself." The prospect immediately feels heard — and didn't have to say a word yet.
Prospect
"Yeah, exactly. That's the biggest thing. I just — I don't know how to handle it without feeling like I'm being pushy."
Already in the real conversation. 45 seconds in. Discovery didn't waste 10 minutes getting to this point.
Closer
"The pushiness feeling is actually where we need to start — because that feeling is not about what you're saying. It's about what you believe about yourself when you're asking. Tell me more about what happens in your head when they say that."
The handoff enabled a precise, high-trust opening. The closer is already working the real problem. The call will close at a higher rate because the first minute didn't get wasted on logistics.
Lesson 9 · Section 10 · Coach: Jalen Hoxie

Setting Metrics

Connect rate. Booking rate. Show rate. These three numbers tell you everything about where your setting system is broken — and which lever to pull first.

The 3 Core Setter Metrics

Every setter performance problem traces back to one of three metrics. Connect rate tells you if you're reaching people. Booking rate tells you what happens when you do. Show rate tells you what happens between booking and call.

Fixing the wrong metric is expensive. A setter with a great booking rate but a broken show rate doesn't need better opening lines — they need a better confirmation sequence. Understanding which number is broken tells you exactly what to fix.

Connect Rate
Target: 15-25% of outreach attempts
The percentage of outreach attempts (DMs sent, calls dialed) that result in an actual back-and-forth conversation — not just a reply or a pickup, but genuine engagement. A "yes" to a calendar link from a cold DM doesn't count as a connect if there was no real conversation first.
Booking Rate
Target: 30-50% of real conversations
Of the people you actually have a conversation with, what percentage book a call? This measures the quality of your setting conversation itself — your hook, your qualification, your bridge, your ask. A low booking rate means the conversation isn't working. The opener might be fine but the bridge to a call is breaking down.
Show Rate
Target: 70-85% of booked calls
Of the calls that get booked, what percentage actually show up? This is almost entirely a function of your confirmation system and lead quality. A high booking rate with a low show rate usually means either your confirmation sequence is weak or you're booking people who weren't actually ready for a call.

Connect Rate — What Good Looks Like

What It Measures

Connect rate tells you whether your outreach is reaching the right people through the right channels. If your connect rate is below 10%, the problem isn't your opener — it's who you're reaching out to or how you're finding them.

Connect rate can also be depressed by the wrong timing (cold calling outside business hours), the wrong platform (LinkedIn vs. cold call vs. Instagram), or targeting prospects who are too senior and screen all unknown contacts.

Connect Rate Diagnosis Fix
Below 5% Wrong audience, wrong channel, or wrong time Audit your targeting. Are you reaching people who match the ICP? Is the channel right for this prospect type?
5-15% Reach is okay but opener may be triggering sales guard Review first message on LinkedIn or first 10 seconds on cold call. Is it triggering the "sales call" pattern?
15-25% Healthy connect rate Focus on converting connects to bookings (booking rate).
Above 25% Strong connect rate — possible over-indexing on warm list Check if you're only reaching out to easy contacts. Great connect rate + low booking rate = not qualifying well.

Booking Rate and Show Rate — Diagnosing the Break

Low Booking Rate — What's Breaking

If you're connecting with people but not converting to calls, the problem is inside the conversation itself. Either you're pitching too fast (triggering rejection), not qualifying enough (running out of things to say), not bridging naturally to the call (asking for the calendar too abruptly), or not creating enough genuine interest before the ask.

Fix: Record your conversations. Listen for where the energy shifts. The moment they start pulling back is the moment your conversation broke. Work backwards from there.

Low Show Rate — What's Breaking

If you're booking calls but they're not showing up, the problem is almost never lead quality alone — it's the post-booking system. You booked someone who was excited in the moment but not invested enough to hold the commitment 48 hours later. The confirmation sequence (Lesson 7) is the primary fix for a broken show rate.

Secondary issue: you may be booking people who didn't fully qualify. Someone who books out of politeness, not genuine interest, has a very high no-show rate.

Don't fix the wrong metric. A setter with 20% connect rate, 40% booking rate, and 55% show rate has a show rate problem — not a connect or booking problem. Spending time on opener refinement won't fix a confirmation system that isn't working.

Exchange — Metrics Review Coaching Session

COACHING CALL — Jalen Diagnosing a Setter's Numbers
Coach (Jalen)
"Walk me through your last two weeks. How many outreach attempts, how many real conversations, how many books, how many shows?"
Setter
"I sent about 120 DMs on LinkedIn, got maybe 18 replies that actually went somewhere, booked 9 calls, and 4 showed up."
120 outreach. 18 connects (15%). 9 books (50% of connects). 4 shows (44% of books). Connect and booking rates are solid. Show rate is broken.
Coach (Jalen)
"Your connect rate and booking rate are actually solid. 15% connect, 50% booking — you're setting the call well. The issue is entirely post-booking. What does your confirmation sequence look like right now?"
Setter
"I send them the calendar link and then I remind them the day before."
Coach (Jalen)
"That's the problem. You have two touchpoints — you need three, and none of them should be generic. The immediate message after booking needs to reference what they told you. The 24-hour message needs to anchor the cost of not showing up. The day-of message needs to give them a reschedule path. You're not losing these people because the set was bad. You're losing them because you went cold after the book."
The metrics diagnosed the problem precisely. No guessing. The fix is specific: improve the confirmation sequence, not the opener or the hook.
Lesson 10 · Section 10

Volume vs. Quality

The KPIs that actually matter for setters. Volume without quality is noise. Quality without volume is unsustainable. Here's where the line is and how to stay on the right side of it.

The Volume Trap

The volume trap: A setter books 25 calls in a week. The closer takes 18 of them. 3 close. The team celebrates the setter's "high volume." But the closer burned out on 15 terrible calls and is now questioning their own close rate.

Volume metrics look good on paper and feel productive in the moment. They also mask a quality problem that quietly destroys closer performance and morale over time.

A bad lead isn't neutral. It costs the closer an hour, it burns state, and if it happens consistently, it starts warping the closer's belief in the program. The setter who books unqualified leads is not performing well — they're creating damage.

The KPIs That Actually Matter

Show Rate
Target: 80%+
If show rate drops below 70%, the leads being booked aren't serious enough. Booking confirmation is weak or qualification was skipped.
Closer Close Rate on Setter's Leads
Target: Matches overall close rate
If the closer's close rate drops on a specific setter's leads, the quality is the problem — not the closer.
Booking Rate from Conversations
Target: 30-50%
What % of genuine conversations turn into booked calls. Below 20% = weak qualification or positioning. Above 60% = possibly not qualifying hard enough.
Revenue Generated per Lead Booked
The real number
The setter's actual contribution metric. Not "calls booked" — revenue closed from booked calls. Volume means nothing without this.

The Quality Floor — What Must Be True Before a Call Books

Don't book ifDo book when
They can't articulate why they're interestedThey have a clear and specific reason for the call
No indication they can or would invest at the program levelBudget capacity is either confirmed or reasonably inferred
Third-party decision maker not identified ("my wife would have to agree" with no plan to include her)Decision maker is on the call or their role in the decision is understood
They're "just curious" with no urgencyThere's a clear timeline or pain driving the inquiry
They booked to get the setter off the phoneThey booked because they genuinely want the conversation

Sustainable Volume

The goal is not to book fewer calls. It's to book the right calls at a volume that's repeatable. A setter who books 8 genuinely qualified calls per week is more valuable than one who books 20 mixed-quality calls — every time.

Volume for its own sake is ego. It feels productive. It looks good in the daily standup. And it slowly erodes the team's results, morale, and belief in the product. Quality is a form of respect for the closer's time and energy.

The right target: hit minimum quality standards on every call, then push volume within those constraints. Don't loosen the quality floor to hit a volume target. Ever.

S11
Peak Performance
Lesson 1 · Section 11

The 2-Minute Pre-Call Check

Your state is your strategy. Who you are when you dial is the most important variable on the call. This protocol makes sure you show up as the right version of yourself every single time.

What the Pre-Call Check Is

The Protocol

Two minutes. Before every call. Not ten minutes, not a full ritual — two minutes to run a quick internal audit and confirm you're actually ready for the person about to pick up the phone.

Most reps finish one call and immediately dial the next. They carry the energy of the last conversation directly into this one — the lost deal, the no-show, the difficult objection — and the prospect has no idea what happened before them. But they feel it. That residue is costing you closes you should be winning.

This check is not about hyping yourself up. It's not a motivation ritual. It's a calibration — a quick read on where you are right now and whether that state serves the next person on the call.

If you're calibrated, you dial. If you're not, you take 60 more seconds and run the reset. Then you dial. That's the whole protocol.

Why reps skip it: They tell themselves they don't have time. The reality is they don't have time not to. A 2-minute check prevents a 60-minute call from going sideways. The math is obvious. The discipline is what separates the reps who do it from the reps who stay inconsistent.

The 5 Questions

Run through these before every call. Honest answers only — this doesn't work if you tell yourself what you want to hear.

01
Question One
What level am I at right now?
On a 1-10 scale, where are you? Not where you want to be — where you actually are. If you're honest and the number is below a 7, something needs to shift before you dial. You cannot run a high-level conversation from a 4.
Below a 7 means you run the reset protocol before dialing. Non-negotiable. A 6 feels close enough but it isn't.
02
Question Two
What do I know about this person?
30 seconds of context before you dial. What's their situation? What did they come in saying? Job, income range, stated goal. You should never dial someone you haven't looked at. Even a quick CRM scan counts. You can't be genuinely curious about someone you know nothing about.
If you know nothing about them, spend 60 seconds in Close before the call. Don't show up blank. Blank is disrespectful to the call and to the prospect.
03
Question Three
What am I genuinely curious about?
Name one real thing you want to understand about this person. Not a discovery question from a playbook — an actual thing. "I want to know why they haven't pulled the trigger yet" or "I want to understand what's kept them stuck." Genuine curiosity changes how you listen and how the conversation feels to them.
If you can't name one real curiosity, you're about to pitch at them instead of have a conversation with them. Pitching at people doesn't close. Conversations do.
04
Question Four
What do I want for them?
Not what do you want from them — what do you want for them. Do you genuinely want this person to get clarity on whether this is the right move? Or are you here to close a deal? These are different orientations and the prospect can feel both of them. One builds trust, the other erodes it.
Correct answer: "I want this person to leave clearer than they arrived." If your honest answer is "I want to hit my number," run the reset before dialing.
05
Question Five
Am I okay if this doesn't close today?
This is the hardest one to answer honestly. If the deal doesn't happen on this call, are you actually okay? Not "yeah sure" okay — genuinely, operationally okay. If you're not, that need will show up on the call in your pace, your tone, and your responses to friction. And it will cost you the deal.
The goal is non-attachment, not indifference. You care about the outcome — you just don't need it. The moment you need it, you've already lost leverage.

The Reset Protocol

When Your Answers Aren't Where They Need to Be

If any of the five questions surfaces a problem — your state is low, you're not curious, you need this close — you don't push through it. You take 60 additional seconds and run the reset. This is how you stop carrying yesterday's losses into today's opportunities.

  • ✓
    Physical state first. Stand up. Take three slow breaths from your abdomen, not your chest. Roll your shoulders back and down. Your physical posture directly affects your mental state. Do the physical piece before anything mental.
  • ✓
    Release the last call. If you're carrying residue from a difficult conversation, name it and set it down. "That call didn't go how I wanted. It's over. This is a completely different person in a completely different situation." That's it. Don't process the last call now — just release it.
  • ✓
    Anchor to your intention. Say this out loud if you can: "My job is to find out what's true for this person and help them make the right decision." This single sentence resets your orientation from closer to advisor. Advisors don't need the sale — they need the right answer for the person in front of them.
  • ✓
    Find one genuine curiosity. Pull up something you're actually curious about before you dial. Even one question. Curiosity is the fastest bridge from anxious and outcome-attached to present and effective.

If after the reset you're still not in a state that serves the prospect: Consider whether you need a longer break. A call taken in the wrong state produces outcomes worse than rescheduling. Know this. Use it. Rescheduling one call to protect your state on the next five is the right call.

The Rep Who Does This vs. The Rep Who Doesn't

Variable Skips the Check Runs the Check
Opening energy Carries residue from the last call — too amped, too flat, or mildly reactive Enters calibrated — present, curious, grounded in the right intention
Discovery quality Goes through the motions; questions feel mechanical because they're not actually curious Asks from genuine interest; listens differently when they actually want the answer
Handling objections Reactive; feels threatening because they need the close Steady; objections are information, not a signal the deal is dying
Performance over time Inconsistent; results driven by mood and what happened on the last call More consistent; performance driven by a repeatable internal protocol

"Your state is your strategy. Who you are when you dial is the most important variable on the call."

— JJ Hernandez
The Bottom Line

The pre-call check is not a nicety. It's not a soft skill. It's the most direct lever you have over your close rate that isn't dependent on what the prospect says or does. You control your state. Most reps act like they don't. That's the gap.

Two minutes. Every call. No exceptions.

Lesson 2 · Section 11

The Consciousness Levels

Where you're operating from determines what's possible on the call. This is the Map of Consciousness applied directly to sales performance. Know your level. Know how to move it.

Why This Applies to Call Performance

The Concept (From S1 Lesson 15)

In Section 1 you studied the Map of Consciousness — a hierarchy of emotional operating states from shame and guilt at the bottom through courage and neutrality in the middle to love and enlightenment at the top. That was the theory. This lesson is the practical application to your sales calls specifically.

Where you are on that map when you dial determines everything — your tone, your patience, your curiosity, your ability to hold silence, your reaction to objections, your close rate.

The practical range for sales performance is narrow: Desire (125) through Neutrality (250). Below desire is apathy and fear. Above neutrality starts to become abstract for daily performance. Your job is to identify which level you're operating from and, when needed, move up one level before the call starts.

The Core Insight

You cannot operate above your current state. A rep running from Fear cannot choose to access Courage on a call just because they want to. The state is operating the rep — the rep is not operating the state. This is why catching and moving your level before the call matters more than any technique you'll ever learn.

The Four Levels You'll Operate From

These are the states most reps cycle through on a daily basis. Learn to identify which one is running you right now.

Desire · 125
Wanting the Close
This is the most common low state in sales. It feels like motivation — "I want to close this deal." But Desire is energetically needy. It has an agenda. The prospect feels that agenda and it makes them guard their decision. Reps running from Desire fill silences, rush pitches, over-explain, and drop prices without being asked. The tell: you feel subtly desperate even when you're performing confidence.
Pride · 175
Needing to Be Right
Pride is better than Desire but still below the floor. Reps in Pride argue instead of listen, get defensive when challenged, and can't let objections land without countering immediately. The tell: you feel a flash of irritation when a prospect questions your credibility or your program. That irritation is Pride. It closes the conversational loop instead of opening it.
Courage · 200
The Floor for High Performance
Courage is the minimum viable state for a sales call. From Courage, you can ask the uncomfortable question, hold the silence, push back respectfully when needed, and not collapse when the prospect pushes back on you. You're not playing it safe. You're not holding back the real question. You say what needs to be said because you're committed to finding the truth of their situation, not just what's comfortable.
Neutrality · 250
The Optimal Operating State
Neutrality is not indifference. It's the state where outcomes don't run you. You care about the person — genuinely — but you're not attached to what they decide. From Neutrality, objections are information rather than threats. Silence is comfortable rather than terrifying. "No" is data rather than rejection. This is where the best closers live. The prospect feels it as unshakeable calm combined with genuine care. It's the most magnetic state you can bring to a call.

How to Move Up a Level in Real Time

Not meditation. Not journaling. Specific actions that shift your operating level before or during a call.

D→C
Desire to Courage
Stop needing and start deciding
When you're in Desire, your focus is on the outcome. Shift it deliberately to the process. Say this out loud: "I am going to ask the questions that need to be asked and let this person tell me the truth." That's a Courage move. You're committing to the right action regardless of the outcome.
Physical anchor: Stand up straight. Take one slow breath. The posture of someone in Courage is different from the posture of someone in Desire. Change the body first.
P→C
Pride to Courage
Get curious about what you're resisting
Pride contracts. Courage opens. When you feel defensiveness rising — toward a prospect who challenges you, toward a question that feels like an attack — the move is to get curious about the defensiveness itself. "Why am I feeling the need to defend this?" Usually it's because you're not confident in your own conviction. Curiosity dissolves Pride.
Ask this: "What would I say if I had nothing to prove?" Then say that.
C→N
Courage to Neutrality
Release the outcome completely
Courage still has a stake in the outcome — it just doesn't hide from it. Neutrality releases the stake. The move: remind yourself that your job is to tell the truth about what this person needs and let them decide. Whether they buy or don't is their decision. Your job is just to make sure they have everything they need to make it well. That release is Neutrality.
Ground phrase: "I am here to serve the truth of this conversation, not to manage the outcome." Say it before you dial. Mean it.

The Reset Moves — Catching Yourself Mid-Call

When You Notice You've Dropped Levels on a Call

You won't always enter a call at Neutrality or Courage. And even when you do, the call can pull you down — a difficult objection, a dismissive tone, a direct challenge. The skill is not starting at Neutrality. It's catching when you've dropped and moving back up while the call is happening.

  • !
    You catch yourself in Desire mid-call. The tell: you're talking too fast, adding unprompted justifications, or internally calculating how close you are to the close. Reset: pause. Ask an open question. The act of asking and genuinely waiting for the answer breaks the Desire pattern and pulls you back into presence.
  • !
    You catch yourself in Pride mid-call. The tell: you're subtly debating, you just countered an objection before you let it fully land, or you feel a need to prove something. Reset: say "tell me more about that" and mean it. Invite them deeper into their objection. The act of genuine invitation dissolves Pride.
  • ✓
    The universal mid-call reset. Slow down your speech by about 20%. Take a slightly longer pause before your next response. Ask one genuinely curious question.
