L1
Level 1
Engaged but Cautious
Prospect is interested, asks questions, gives real answers in discovery. Mentions they've "tried other programs" once. Agrees to the price without major resistance. This level tests basic structure and discovery depth.
L2
Level 2
One Hard Objection
Prospect gives surface answers in discovery until pushed. One firm objection during close — "I need to think about it" or "the price is more than I expected." Coach holds the objection for at least 30 seconds to test whether the rep rushes or gets curious.
L3
Level 3
Credibility Challenge + Price
Prospect questions how long the rep has been doing this, references a competitor, and then objects to the price. Tests composure under credibility attack and ability to hold frame through a two-stage objection sequence.
L4
Level 4
Closed Off + Multiple Objections
Prospect gives one-word answers in early discovery. Multiple objections stacked at close. Tests whether the rep can open a closed prospect and maintain composure through sustained resistance.
L5
Level 5
Full Gauntlet
Hostile at opener, closed in discovery, credibility challenge mid-call, price objection, "I need to talk to my partner," and one more unknown variable the coach adds at their discretion. The full gauntlet. If the rep can hold frame and stay curious through this, real calls feel manageable.

Recording and Reviewing — The Compound Effect

The mock call is 50% of the value. The review is the other 50%. A mock call without a review is a slightly more realistic conversation. A mock call with a review is a targeted training intervention that directly improves your next real call.

Review the Mock Using the 5-Point Framework

Same framework as a real call. Opener, Discovery, Pitch, Objection Handling, Close. Score each point 1-3. Identify the lowest score. That's what the next mock targets. Run the next mock specifically against that weakness.

  • ✓
    Minimum 2 mock calls per week in the first 90 days. This is not optional. It's the difference between reps who plateau and reps who break through. Two mocks a week, reviewed with the 5-Point Framework, applied to real calls.
  • ✓
    Start at Level 2 difficulty minimum. Level 1 is for day one of a brand-new rep only. Anyone who has been on at least 10 real calls should start at Level 2 and move up based on performance.
  • ✓
    Apply one thing per mock to the next real call. The transfer is mandatory. What did you learn? What are you changing? State it out loud after the review. Do it on the next call.
  • ✗
    Don't debrief mid-call. Coach stays in character until the call ends. Post-call debrief happens after a 5-minute break. Breaking the mock to coach kills the flow and realism that makes the practice valuable.
Lesson 7 · Section 12

Frame Attack Drill

Stress-testing your frame under consecutive pressure. Not an argument drill. A state drill. You win when you stay grounded and curious — not when you "win" the exchange.

What the Frame Attack Drill Is

One partner fires consecutive challenges — objections, credibility attacks, pressure statements — one after another with no pause. The other partner holds state: grounded, curious, unhurried. No scripts. No canned rebuttals. Just state management under fire.

The goal is not to have the perfect answer. The goal is to not break. To stay in your body. To respond from curiosity instead of defense. To maintain the same tone at challenge 5 that you had at challenge 1.

Why This Drill Exists

Most reps can hold frame for one objection. Maybe two. By challenge three, they're rushing, defending, getting louder, or completely conceding. The Frame Attack Drill isolates state management from everything else on a call. No need to track where you are in the conversation, no prospect relationship to manage — just consecutive pressure and your response to it.

Five rounds a week builds a different rep. One who doesn't flinch at the fourth objection on a real call because they've trained at level 5 in the drill.

5 Levels of Escalating Difficulty

Level 1
Soft Resistance
Attacker uses gentle pushback. "I'm not sure this is for me." "I've been thinking about whether I'm ready." "The price seems high." Three challenges, low intensity, no personal edge. Goal: establish the baseline of staying grounded and curious on mild resistance. Most reps pass Level 1 immediately.
Level 2
Consecutive Objections
Five consecutive objections with no validation between them. Price, timing, skepticism about results, comparison to a competitor, another price. No breaks. Attacker doesn't pause or wait for a complete answer — moves to next challenge after 8-10 seconds. Tests whether the rep speeds up or stays measured across a sustained run of resistance.
Level 3
Credibility Attacks
"How long have you been doing this?" "Have you actually been a sales rep or just a coach?" "My last coach told me the exact same thing and it didn't work." "Why should I believe your results are real?" Personal credibility is attacked. The rep must respond without becoming defensive, without listing credentials, and without apologizing. Staying curious here is genuinely difficult.
Level 4
Personal and Emotional
Attacker uses language designed to trigger ego: "This feels like a high-pressure sales tactic." "You sound like every other coach I've talked to." "I don't think you actually understand my situation." "I don't think this is going to work for me." These hit differently than price objections. They're designed to make the rep feel unseen or disrespected. The trained response is the same: slow down, get curious, don't defend.
Level 5
Full Gauntlet
Eight consecutive challenges mixing all four types: soft resistance, consecutive objections, credibility attacks, and personal/emotional hits. No pattern. The rep cannot predict what comes next. Duration: 3-4 minutes of continuous pressure. If the rep can complete Level 5 with consistent tone, no defensive acceleration, and at least one genuine curiosity response per challenge — they're ready for anything a real call can produce.

What Success and Failure Look Like

Frame Breaking — What Failure Sounds Like
Attacker
"Your results sound like every other coaching program I've heard about. I've done three of these and gotten nothing."
Rep
"No no, I totally get that — but we're really different, we actually have results to back this up, like I can show you testimonials, our method is completely different from anything else out there, JJ has worked with multiple eight-figure companies and our students consistently get results, plus we have a guarantee so there's literally no risk..."
Pace accelerated. Volume increased. Defensive list of credentials. Introduced "guarantee" unprompted. Zero curiosity about what those other experiences were like. Frame fully broken.
Frame Held — What Success Sounds Like
Attacker
"Your results sound like every other coaching program I've heard about. I've done three of these and gotten nothing."
Rep
[2-second pause] "Three programs. That's a real pattern. Tell me what those were like — what did each one promise you'd get, and what actually happened?"
Same pace. Same tone. Didn't defend. Got curious. Now the attacker has to explain their actual experience — which surfaces the real objection. Frame intact.
Attacker
"They all taught the same scripts. Generic stuff. Nothing connected to how I actually talk."
Rep
"That's exactly what this isn't. And I hear that skepticism — three programs is enough to earn it. What would have to be true about this for it to feel different to you?"
Acknowledged the skepticism without validating the conclusion. Invited them to define what would work. Curiosity sustained through pressure. Level 3 drill passed.

Running the Drill and Reviewing It

Setup

Two reps. Record every session. The attacker fires from the level chosen — no easing in, no warmup. Start at the agreed level immediately. The defender does not know what's coming next. Duration: 3-5 minutes per round. Switch roles. Both people need both sides of this drill.

Reviewing the Drill on Tape

After the session, review the recording together. Listen for: pace changes, volume changes, defensive language, filler words that appeared under pressure, the moment curiosity gave way to arguing. The reviewer gives one observation per challenge — specific, timestamped, behavioral. The defender commits to one change before the next session.

  • ✓
    Success = staying grounded and curious, not winning the argument. If you "won" by out-arguing the attacker, you failed the drill. The metric is state, not outcome.
  • ✓
    Frequency: twice weekly for reps with a frame ceiling. Once you consistently pass Level 4 without breaking, move to weekly maintenance.
  • ✗
    Failure signals: defensive speed increase, volume change, conceding before being asked, introducing unprompted justifications. Any of these mean the frame broke. That's what to fix next session.
  • ✗
    Don't debate about the "right" answer mid-drill. There is no right answer in the Frame Attack Drill. There is only state. Stay in state. That's the only metric.

"I'd rather lose the sale than lose the frame. Because if you lose the frame, you lose the sale anyway — and you lose your self-respect along with it."

— JJ Hernandez
Lesson 8 · Section 12

The Integration Mock Call

The capstone practice session. Every framework you've learned must be running simultaneously. This is not a drill for one skill — this is the whole thing at once.

What Integration Actually Means

Most reps can run frameworks one at a time. They can hold frame when they're thinking about holding frame. They can go deep in discovery when they remind themselves to go deep. They can deliver a connected pitch when that's what they're focused on.

Integration means all of it is running at the same time without deliberate effort. You're not thinking "go deeper." You're just curious. You're not thinking "hold frame." You're just grounded. The frameworks stop being techniques and become expressions of who you are on the call.

The Gap This Exposes

Most reps discover in the integration mock that they can only run two or three frameworks simultaneously before something falls apart. They get deep in discovery and lose track of frame. They pitch well and then concede on the close. They hold frame through objections and forget to use the prospect's own language.

That gap — between running frameworks one at a time and running all of them simultaneously — is the difference between a good rep and an elite one.

The Scoring Rubric — All Frameworks Present

The integration mock is reviewed against every dimension simultaneously. Coach scores each in real time during the call, then reviews with the rep afterward.

Structure — The 12 Steps
Were all 12 steps of the Sales.io call structure present?
Did the call move through every phase? Were any skipped? Which step was weakest?
Missing Partial Complete
Influence — S7 Principles Running
Were the influence principles from Section 7 present throughout the call?
Social proof, cognitive dissonance, identity-reality gap — did they appear naturally or not at all?
Absent Forced Natural
Frame — Intact Throughout
Was the frame held from opener to close, including through all objections?
Note every moment the frame wavered. At what point, if any, did the rep concede the advisor position?
Broke Down Wavered Held
State — Above 200 Throughout
Was the rep's emotional state above the line of reason (not fear, not desperation) for the whole call?
Using Dr. Hawkins' scale as reference: 200+ means courage and above. Below 200 is fear, shame, guilt, apathy — states that leak through tone and kill trust. Was the rep above 200 the whole call?
Below 200 Fluctuating Above 200
Discovery — Third Layer Reached
Did the rep reach at least the third layer of the prospect's pain before pitching?
Layer 1: surface statement. Layer 2: why that matters. Layer 3: what it costs them — emotionally, financially, relationally. Did the pitch connect to layer 3 content?
Layer 1 Only Layer 2 Layer 3+
Overall Integration Score
Across all dimensions — how integrated was this call?
Integration 3 means all frameworks ran simultaneously without the rep visibly thinking about them. Integration 1 means frameworks were isolated or absent. This is the most important single score.
1 — Not Integrated 2 — Partial 3 — Fully Integrated

What Integration Feels Like

Running Frameworks One at a Time Full Integration
You're thinking about the next step while the prospect is talking. Missing what they're saying. You're fully present. The frameworks are background. You're just in a conversation.
When you focus on discovery depth, your frame gets soft. One or the other. Deep discovery and solid frame are happening simultaneously. Not a trade-off.
Transitions feel mechanical. "Now I should go to the pitch." Transitions feel natural. The call moves because the conversation moves, not because a script says so.
You notice the prospect's emotions after the fact. Reacting late. You're reading the prospect in real time. Adjusting your approach inside the moment.
The Integration Feeling

When full integration is happening, the call feels easy in a way that's hard to explain. Not easy because the prospect isn't pushing back — easy because you're not fighting yourself anymore. You're not managing your frameworks. You're just there. The close feels natural because the whole call built toward it.

That's what you're training for. Not a better technique — a different way of being on a call.

Why This Separates Good Reps from Elite Ones

Good reps have strong frameworks. Elite reps have integrated frameworks. The difference is not technique — it's bandwidth. When you're not spending cognitive energy managing your process, that bandwidth goes to the prospect. And what the prospect feels is someone who is entirely, genuinely with them.

That's what connection feels like from the other side. Not a technique. Not a rapport tactic. Someone who is fully present and completely certain — because all the internal noise is gone.

  • !
    The integration mock is not for beginners. Run this after the individual framework mocks and Frame Attack Drill have been completed. Integration cannot happen if the component skills aren't built yet.
  • ✓
    Run one integration mock per week in the final 4 weeks of the program. This is the calibration check — all systems running together before going fully live with the skills.
  • ✓
    Record and review every integration mock. The integration score is what you're tracking. A consistent score of 3 across all dimensions means you're integrated. That's when the real calls get easy.
  • ✗
    Don't grade yourself on outcome in the integration mock. Whether you "closed" the mock prospect is irrelevant. Score the integration. The close is a byproduct of integrated execution — not the measure of it.

"Everyone else teaches you what to say. We teach you how to think. Integration is when the thinking becomes invisible — and that's when the prospect stops hearing a sales rep and starts hearing the truth."

— JJ Hernandez
Lesson 9 · Section 12

Call Submission Protocol

How to submit a call for JJ or coaching staff to review — what to include, how to ask, and how to turn that feedback into a measurable improvement.

How to Submit a Call

Staff call review is one of the highest-leverage tools in the program. You are getting eyes from someone who has reviewed hundreds of calls and can identify patterns in minutes that you might not see in weeks of solo review. Use this resource. And use it correctly.

An unclear submission gets a generic response. A clear, specific submission gets exactly the feedback you need.

01
Step 1
Do Your Own Review First
Before submitting a call, review it yourself. Use the 5-Point Framework (Lesson 3). Complete the worksheet. Identify the moment you think the call went sideways. Do not submit a call you haven't already watched. Submissions that begin with "I haven't listened to it yet but..." get deprioritized. Your own review is not optional — it's the prerequisite.
02
Step 2
Share the Recording Link
Share a Zoom recording link or Loom link with permissions open — the reviewer shouldn't have to request access. If the call is over 60 minutes, also include a timestamp range for the most important section. A full 90-minute call with no direction is harder to give useful feedback on than a targeted 20-minute segment.
03
Step 3
Include Your Own Review Notes
What did you score each of the 5 points? What did you notice? What did you feel good about and what did you feel uncertain about? Your own review notes give the coach context — they can either confirm what you saw, tell you that you missed something bigger, or reframe the thing you noticed entirely. All three are valuable.
04
Step 4
Ask One Specific Question
The most important part of the submission. Not "what do you think?" — that is the question that produces the least useful feedback. One specific question about one specific moment or dimension. The more specific your question, the more actionable the answer.
Good: "At minute 14:30, the prospect said they'd tried another program. I asked about it but felt like I moved on too fast. Did I close that loop well, or was there a better follow-up?"
Not useful: "Let me know what you think overall."

What Makes a Good Submission

Weak Submission Strong Submission
"Here's my call. Let me know what you think." "Here's my review notes from the 5-Point Framework. My weakest score was Discovery (2/3). At minute 9 the prospect said X and I moved on — I think I left a layer unturned. Did I?"
No recording link, or link requires permission request. Open link, timestamp range provided for the specific moment in question.
Submitted without having reviewed the call first. Submitted with a completed 5-Point worksheet and at least two specific observations the rep already made.
"What should I have done differently?" "I chose to go to the close at minute 22 even though there were two unresolved objections. Was that the right call, or should I have gone back into discovery first?"
The Specificity Principle

The more specific your question, the more precise the feedback. "What do you think of my call?" invites a general overview — useful, but diffuse. "At minute 18, did my tone stay grounded through the price objection or did I speed up?" invites a targeted observation that you can immediately apply. Specific question, specific answer, specific change.

The Submission Template

Use this every time. Copy it, fill it in, paste it into the coaching channel with your recording link.

Call Date and Outcome
When was the call? What was the result? (closed / no close / follow-up)
Context the reviewer needs upfront.
Recording Link
Paste the open-access recording link here. Include timestamp range if relevant.
If the full call is long, note the 10-15 minute window most relevant to your question.
My 5-Point Scores
Opener / Discovery / Pitch / Objection Handling / Close — one score (1-3) for each.
Show your own work. The reviewer might agree, confirm, or reframe your scoring.
What I Noticed
What did you observe in your own review? What felt off? What felt strong?
Two to three sentences. Enough for the reviewer to know your perspective before they watch.
My One Specific Question
The single most important thing you want the reviewer's eyes on — with a timestamp if possible.
This is the most important field. Be specific. One question. The review will address other things too — but this is what you most need answered.

Expected Turnaround and How to Use the Feedback

Expected Turnaround

Staff call reviews are typically returned within 48-72 hours of a complete submission. A complete submission means all fields above are filled in and the recording is accessible. Incomplete submissions take longer or may be returned for more information first.

When you receive feedback: read it once, sit with it, then apply it before your next call. Not after three more calls. The next call. The turnaround on application is what determines whether the feedback compounds or evaporates.

  • ✓
    After receiving feedback, state out loud what you're changing on the next call. Write it on a sticky note if that helps. Application is the only metric that matters after a review.
  • ✓
    Submit your next call for review with a note about what you tried to change. "Last review, you flagged that I moved on too fast after the first objection. Here's my next call — did the correction land?" This creates a feedback loop. This is how reps improve at 3x speed.
  • ✗
    Don't submit calls just to submit them. One well-prepared submission is worth ten lazy ones. The goal is not volume of submissions — it's the quality of the question and the speed of application.
  • ✗
    Don't defend the feedback internally. If the reviewer says something that stings or that you don't immediately agree with, write it down and sit with it for 24 hours before deciding it's wrong. Feedback from someone who has reviewed hundreds of calls is usually right.
Lesson 10 · Section 12

Building a Monthly Review Habit

Weekly reviews catch noise. Monthly reviews catch signal. The pattern that's been true for 4 consecutive weeks is the real thing you need to fix — and monthly review is the only way to see it.

Why Monthly Review Is Different

Weekly reviews are reactive — they tell you what happened this week. They're subject to variance: one unusually strong week can hide a problem, one bad week can cause panic when the trend is actually fine.

Monthly review is pattern-level. It averages out the variance and reveals what's consistently true about your calls. One bad close is a data point. Four consecutive bad closes on the same step is a pattern — and patterns are fixable in a way single events aren't.

Weekly review revealsMonthly review reveals
What happened this weekWhat's been consistently true for the past 4 weeks
Good or bad weeks (variance)Actual trend direction
This week's close rateTrue close rate ceiling
Immediate fixesStructural problems requiring real work

The Monthly Review Protocol

  • Pull the data. Close rate, show rate, deal size, objection frequency — pull the last 4 weekly reviews and look at them together. What's the trend in close rate? Is it going up, down, or flat?
  • Find the consistent pattern. What objection came up every week? What step did you score lowest in every week? The things that show up repeatedly are your real constraints — not one-off events.
  • Name the one thing. Not three things. One. The one constraint that, if removed, would have the largest effect on your results. This becomes your focus for the next month.
  • Set the drill. What specific practice or habit will you run every day next month to address the one thing? Not "work on discovery" — "Ask one deeper question after every surface answer. Every call. Review whether I did it after each call."
  • Set a baseline number. What do you expect your close rate to be next month if the one thing improves? Write it down. This is your target — not a goal to hit, but a hypothesis to test.

What Monthly Review Has Revealed (Common Findings)

The most common monthly review finding: The close rate was fine for weeks 1-3 and then collapsed in week 4. Almost always, this is a state management issue — fatigue, overconfidence after a good streak, or life circumstances bleeding into the calls.

Second most common: The close rate is consistent, but average deal size has been dropping. This usually means the prospect quality is declining (setter issue), or the rep is defaulting to split pay without exploring why.

Third most common: The rep thinks they're losing deals to objections, but the data shows most deals go quiet after the pitch — before any objection is raised. This is almost always a discovery problem: the pitch didn't connect, which means discovery didn't surface the right pain.

Making It a Habit

Schedule it. Last Sunday of every month, 90 minutes. Same time, same format, every month. The reps who do this consistently are the ones whose close rate trends upward over a year. The ones who skip it plateau.

The goal of monthly review is not to feel bad about what went wrong. It's to find the one lever that's available to pull. One lever, applied consistently for 30 days, compounded over 12 months — that's the growth path. Most reps try to pull 5 levers at once and move none of them.

S13
Finding Roles
Lesson 1 · Section 13

The Sales.io Job Board

4-6 vetted postings drop every day. Most reps glance and move on. The ones who move on it same day are the ones who land roles.

What the Job Board Is

The Sales.io job board is not a job aggregator. It is not LinkedIn. It is not Indeed. Every company posted has been through our vetting process before it appears. That means you are not starting from scratch every time you see a new listing. The floor has already been raised.

4-6 new vetted postings per day. Live roles, active pipelines, companies that are currently hiring closers and setters right now.

What This Replaces

Most reps waste 80% of their job-search time applying to roles on platforms with zero quality filter. They end up at companies that don't pay on time, have no real pipeline, or lie about close rate data. That is not a rep problem. That is a sourcing problem. The job board solves sourcing so you can spend your time on execution.

Who Posts Here

Operators and offer owners in B2C, B2B, coaching, agency, and software verticals. The common thread: proven revenue, real pipeline, and a defined commission structure. The specifics of each criterion are covered in lesson 2.

What's in Each Posting

Every listing includes the same core data points. Know what to look for before you read a single word of the description.

  • ✓
    Offer vertical and product type. B2C, B2B, SaaS, coaching, agency. Know which vertical you perform best in. Start there.
  • ✓
    Deal size and commission structure. What is the average deal? What is the commission percentage or flat fee? If these are missing or vague, that is worth noting before you apply.
  • ✓
    Current close rate (when available). Some operators share it. A 15%+ close rate on warm inbound is solid. A 2% close rate deserves serious questions before you commit.
  • ✓
    Role type: closer, setter, or both. Some postings are pure closing. Some are hybrid. Know what you are applying for before you reach out.
  • ✓
    Lead source and volume. Where do leads come from? How many calls per week? This tells you the actual earning potential. Commission rate means nothing without lead volume behind it.

The Daily Job Board Routine

01
Every Morning
Check the Board First
Not every other day. Not when you feel like it. Every morning. New postings drop daily and roles fill fast. The rep who checks on day 3 is applying to a role already filled by the rep who checked on day 1.
02
Evaluate
Shortlist 2-3 Opportunities Max
You are not applying to everything. Read the posting, check the data, decide if this is a realistic fit. Shortlist 2-3. Anything more dilutes your effort and the quality of your Loom drops immediately.
03
Execute
Move on It Same Day
This is the one that separates reps who get discovery calls from reps who don't. Good operators are actively hiring. If they get a sharp Loom from another rep today and yours arrives four days later, they are already deep into interviews. Same day. Every time.

"You're not applying for a job. You're evaluating a business partner. The rep who interviews the company gets the offer. The rep who begs for the offer doesn't."

— JJ Hernandez

The Competitive Advantage

Every rep in this program sees the same board. That is the level playing field. What is not level is execution speed. Most reps check the board, think "I'll come back to that later," and never move on it. Or they move three days late. Or they apply with a generic text message and wonder why nobody responds.

The rep who moves same day, with a targeted Loom, citing something specific about that company, wins. Not because they are more talented. Because they show up differently than everyone else on that list.

Most RepsYour Approach
See the posting, bookmark it, come back 2-3 days later See the posting, shortlist it, move on it same day
Send a text message or copy-paste resume link Record a 60-second Loom specific to that company
Apply to 10+ roles with no research or specificity Apply to 2-3 roles with full research and a targeted pitch
Wait to hear back passively Follow up once at 48 hours, once at 96 hours, then move on clean

Common Mistakes

Applying to roles outside your current level. If you are new, applying to roles that require 18 months of remote high-ticket experience is not hustle. It is bad targeting. The board has roles for reps at every level. Apply to roles where you are a realistic candidate, then grow from there.

Treating the board like a passive resource. The board is not something you check when you feel like it. It is a live feed of your pipeline for the next 30 days. Your income depends on the offer you are on. Treat this with the same seriousness you treat your close rate.

Sending the same Loom to every company. A generic Loom is just a text application with your face on it. Every Loom needs at least one thing specific to that company. More on this in lessons 7 and 8.

Lesson 2 · Section 13

What Makes a Vetted Offer

Not everything on the board is perfect. But everything on the board has passed a baseline. Understand what that baseline is and how to read above it.

The Vetting Criteria

Before a company appears on the Sales.io job board, it goes through a checklist. These are the four non-negotiables.

  • ✓
    Proven revenue. The company has demonstrated consistent revenue at a meaningful level. We are not posting startup ideas or pre-revenue offers. Reps need a real business behind them to close for.
  • ✓
    Defined commission structure. Commission terms are documented before the posting goes live. No "we'll figure it out" arrangements. The rep knows what they earn before they take the first call.
  • ✓
    Active pipeline. There are real leads coming in right now. Not "we're building our funnel." Not "we'll ramp up in Q3." Active, current, qualified leads in the pipeline today.
  • ✓
    Responsive leadership. The operator or sales manager has demonstrated they communicate and that reps are treated as partners, not disposable labor. This is harder to measure but we check it.

What "Vetted" Means — and What It Doesn't

Vetted is a floor, not a guarantee. It means the basics are in place. It does not mean the offer is perfect for you, the close rate is outstanding, or the operator will never have a bad month.

Your job is to use the vetting as a starting point, then do your own evaluation on top of it. Lessons 3, 5, and 6 cover that in depth.

The Practical Translation

A vetted offer is one where a skilled rep has a real shot at earning strong income. It is not a promise that you will close at 30%. The offer quality creates the conditions. Your skills determine the outcome.

This is the combination that produces consistent income: a solid vetted offer plus a rep who executes at a high level. One without the other breaks down. A great rep on a garbage offer is still working against the current. An average rep on a great offer is leaving money on the table.

The Offer Types Posted

B2C
Business to Consumer
Coaching, courses, masterminds, and transformation programs sold direct to individuals. Typically emotional buying decisions, higher close rates on warm inbound, deal sizes from $2K to $25K+. Most common offer type on the board.
B2B
Business to Business
Selling services, software, or solutions to other businesses. Longer sales cycles, multiple decision-makers, logic-first buying patterns. Higher deal sizes, lower volume. Best for reps who have some experience and are comfortable with consultative selling.
Agency
Done-for-You Services
Marketing agencies, lead generation, social media management, SEO. Usually B2B adjacent. Requires ability to articulate ROI and handle price objections around measurable deliverables.
SaaS
Software as a Service
Subscription-based software products. Deal sizes vary widely. Commission may be based on annual contract value. Good entry point for reps who want to move into tech sales longer term.

Why Vetted Offers Matter for Your Numbers

The Math Is Simple

A rep on a garbage offer calling bad leads will have a 5-8% close rate and wonder what they are doing wrong. A rep with the same skill set on a solid vetted offer calling warm inbound leads will have a 15-25% close rate and wonder why they waited so long to switch.

Your close rate reflects two things: your skill and your offer quality. Most reps only look at one of them.

Do not confuse a low close rate with a skill problem until you have confirmed the offer quality is solid. We see this mistake constantly. Reps grind through training thinking they are the problem when the real issue is the pipeline they are calling or the offer they are closing for. Diagnose the system before you diagnose yourself.

Good offer plus good rep equals predictable income. That is the equation. The board gives you the offer side. Your work in this program gives you the rep side. When both are in place, income stops being a mystery.

Lesson 3 · Section 13

Red Flags vs. Green Flags

Vetting a company before you apply is not optional. It is the difference between a role that builds your close rate and one that destroys it.

Why Vetting Matters

The Real Risk

The danger is not just wasted time. A bad offer damages your close rate and your confidence simultaneously. You can be technically sound, executing everything correctly, and still close at 6% because the pipeline is cold, the offer is weak, or the product has a reputation problem in the market.

Most reps who quit sales do not quit because they lack skill. They quit after six months on a bad offer with a 7% close rate, believing the problem is them. Your due diligence before you take a role protects your career and your mindset.

Every signal you can gather before the discovery call is leverage. You walk into that conversation already knowing what you are looking for. That confidence reads. The rep who researched walks differently than the rep who just showed up.

Red Flags — Walk Away or Probe Hard

  • ✕
    "Unlimited earning potential" with no specific numbers attached. This phrase is the equivalent of "we'll figure it out." Push for actual commission tiers, actual deal sizes, actual rep earnings. If they cannot give you specifics, that tells you something.
  • ✕
    No documented commission structure. Verbal agreements in sales roles disappear the moment a dispute arises. If there is nothing in writing before you start, protect yourself.
  • ✕
    High rep turnover with no explanation. If multiple reps have come and gone in the last 90 days, ask why. "We just haven't found the right fit yet" is not an answer. High turnover usually means pipeline problems, offer problems, or leadership problems.
  • ✕
    No existing close rate data. If a company cannot tell you what their current close rate is, it means either they are not tracking it (red flag for business maturity) or the number is embarrassing (bigger red flag).
  • ✕
    Hostile Glassdoor or public reviews about rep treatment. One bad review from a disgruntled employee is noise. A pattern of reviews citing late payments, misrepresentation, or hostile management is signal.
  • ✕
    Leadership who cannot answer basic business questions. If the operator does not know their monthly revenue, their customer acquisition cost, or what their best reps are making, that company is not ready to support a high-performing closer.

Green Flags — Signs of a Solid Offer

  • ✓
    Transparent, specific commission tiers. The offer comes with documented commission terms. They know the percentages, when payment hits, and what happens with refunds or chargebacks.
  • ✓
    Documented close rate they are proud to share. A company confident in their offer quality will share close rate data without hesitation. When they lead with it, that is a green flag.
  • ✓
    Reps who have been there 6+ months. Rep tenure is one of the most honest indicators of offer quality. Reps stay when they are earning and when they are treated fairly. Longevity is trust.
  • ✓
    Leadership who knows their numbers cold. When the operator or sales manager can immediately cite average deal size, monthly revenue, close rate, and lead volume, you are talking to someone running a real business.
  • ✓
    Genuine testimonials and a product with real reviews. Product quality affects close rate. A product that customers love creates referrals and reduces objections on the call. Look for evidence the product delivers.

Side by Side — Before You Apply

Category Red Flag Green Flag
Earning potential "Unlimited earning potential" — no specifics Specific tiers: "10% on $5K deals; top reps hit $15K-$20K/mo"
Commission structure Verbal agreement, nothing written, "we'll handle it" Written terms before day one — percentages, timing, chargeback policy
Rep tenure 3 reps in 4 months, high turnover, vague explanations Same reps for 6-12 months; can name them and what they earn
Close rate Can't answer, deflects, or gives a suspiciously high number Specific and reasonable — 12-20% on warm inbound is credible
Leadership Can't answer basic business questions; evasive Knows their numbers cold; responds within 24 hours

How to Find These Signals Before the Discovery Call

Before You Even Reach Out

Run the company through the 10-minute research protocol in lesson 5. LinkedIn for leadership and rep tenure. Glassdoor for culture patterns. Google for product reviews and news. You should know whether a company passes or fails the basic checks before you record your Loom.

During the Discovery Call

Use the questions in lesson 6 to surface these signals in conversation. A confident operator with a solid offer will answer everything without hesitation. Watch for evasion, vagueness, or defensiveness. Those responses are data.

Red flags do not mean walk away immediately. They mean probe deeper. Sometimes what looks like a red flag has a reasonable explanation. But if you probe and still cannot get a straight answer, that is your answer.

Lesson 4 · Section 13

The Blacklist

Some companies have already been flagged by reps in this community. Before you apply anywhere, check the list.

How the Blacklist Works

Community-Reported, Internally Verified

The blacklist is maintained by Sales.io based on reports submitted by reps inside the community. When a rep has a documented negative experience with a company, they submit it for review. We verify the claim independently before the company gets added.

This is not a place for grudges or subjective complaints. It is a documentation of patterns that make a company unsuitable for our reps to work with. One bad month from a rep who was not a fit does not land a company on the list. A pattern of behavior against reps does.

The blacklist exists because the community's collective experience is more valuable than any single rep's research. By the time a company gets flagged here, multiple reps have already had the experience you are about to avoid.

Common Blacklist Reasons

01
Payment
Doesn't Pay Commission on Time
The most common blacklist reason. Reps close the deals. Money comes in. Commission does not. The company delays, disputes, or finds reasons to withhold earnings. This is not a misunderstanding. It is a pattern. Companies on the list for this reason have had it verified by multiple reps.
02
Misrepresentation
Lies About Close Rate or Pipeline Quality
The company advertises a 20% close rate. Reps start and find out the real close rate is 6% because leads are old, cold, or not qualified. The misrepresentation may be intentional or the result of leadership not tracking their own data accurately. Either way, reps were misled.
03
Chargebacks
Unusually High Chargeback Rate
When customers refund or dispute at a disproportionate rate, it signals the product is not delivering on its promise. Some companies pass chargeback costs to reps in ways that eliminate earnings. High chargeback rate is also a sign of a product problem, which affects every rep on that offer.
04
Culture
Hostile or Disrespectful to Reps
Leadership that treats reps as interchangeable, blames reps for systemic problems, creates a culture of fear, or publicly humiliates reps in team settings. A rep's performance is partly a function of the environment they work in. Hostile environments produce inconsistent results even from strong closers.
05
Terms
Changes Commission Structure After Reps Start
The original agreement was 10%. After a rep's first good month, the structure quietly changes to 8% with a "new performance threshold" added. These unilateral changes to commission terms after reps are already working are a serious violation of trust and business ethics.

How to Check the Blacklist Before Applying

Step 1 — Check the Pinned List

The blacklist is maintained in the Sales.io community (WhatsApp and Skool). It is pinned in the resources section and updated whenever a new company is added after verification. Check it before you apply to any company, whether it appeared on our board or not.

Step 2 — Search the Community Channel

Before applying to a specific company, run a quick search of the community channel for that company's name. Other reps may have mentioned it in passing even if it has not been formally verified for the blacklist yet. Community intelligence is real-time.

Step 3 — Ask Directly

If you are considering a role and are not sure, post in the community channel and ask. Someone in the 100-200 rep community may have worked there, know someone who did, or have relevant information. You are not in this alone. Use the network.

How to Submit a Company for Review

If you have a documented negative experience with a company, your submission protects the next rep. Submit only what you can verify with evidence. Screenshots of payment disputes, written commission terms that were changed, documented communication patterns. Opinion alone is not enough.

  • ✓
    Document the situation first. Screenshots, written records, dates. The more documented your experience, the faster the verification process moves.
  • ✓
    DM a Sales.io coach or admin. Submit your documentation directly to a coach or program admin in the community. Do not post unverified claims publicly — that protects you and the process.
  • ✓
    We cross-reference with other reps. Before anything goes on the list, we independently verify the pattern with at least one other rep or source.
  • ✕
    Do not post company names publicly before they have been verified. False or premature accusations harm your credibility and expose you to legal risk. Run the process.

Submitting a company does not mean they are blacklisted. It initiates a review. Some submissions reveal misunderstandings that get resolved. That outcome is also valuable. The goal is accurate information, not punishment.

Lesson 5 · Section 13

Research a Company in Under 10 Minutes

You do not need hours of research to know whether a company is worth applying to. You need 10 focused minutes and the right sources.

The 10-Minute Research Protocol

This protocol runs before you record your Loom, not before the discovery call. By the time you are on the call, you have already done this. The research has two purposes: filter out bad fits before you invest time, and give you specifics to reference in your Loom and in your questions on the call.

2m
Source 1
LinkedIn — Founder and Company Page
Search the founder or operator. Check: How long have they been building this company? What is their background? What have they built before? Then check the company page: size, growth trajectory, how long it has existed, how active they are. A company that has been growing steadily for 2+ years is very different from one that launched six weeks ago.
Look for: employee growth (good sign), rep profiles who have been there 6+ months (longevity signal), recent posts from leadership showing business activity.
2m
Source 2
Glassdoor — Culture and Rep Reviews
Search the company on Glassdoor. Not to disqualify them on one bad review, but to identify patterns. Do multiple reviews mention the same issues? Do reviewers mention how long they lasted? What do former sales reps specifically say about commission, pipeline quality, and leadership communication?
One 2-star review from a disgruntled ex-employee is noise. Five reviews over 12 months citing "commissions not paid on time" is signal. Read for patterns, not individual scores.
3m
Source 3
Google — Product Reviews and Press
Search the company name plus "reviews," "complaints," or "scam." Check Trustpilot, Reddit, and any industry forums. Also look for press coverage: were they featured anywhere? Have they been involved in disputes? A product with strong customer reviews is a fundamentally easier sell. A product with a reputation problem is friction on every call you make.
Also search the founder's name directly. Public credibility matters. A founder who is active, respected, and visible in their space gives you talking points in your Loom and makes the offer easier to close.
3m
Source 4
The Offer Page and Sales Materials
Go to the company's website and look at how they present the offer. What claims are they making? What does the customer journey look like? Is the offer positioned clearly, or is it vague? Can you find testimonials? A polished, specific offer page tells you the company takes their product seriously. A generic, low-effort page tells you something different.
Also check their content on Instagram, YouTube, or TikTok. Active content creation signals an engaged operator with a marketing strategy. That means more leads coming in for you to close.

The One Question Your Research Should Answer

After 10 minutes, you should be able to answer one question with confidence: Does this company treat its reps fairly and does its product deliver what it promises?

That is it. Everything else is detail. If you cannot answer that question with at least a provisional yes, you have enough to either probe harder on the discovery call or walk away before you invest more time.

What "Fairly" Actually Means

It means commission is paid on time, as agreed. It means leadership communicates when there are problems. It means reps are not blamed for systemic pipeline or product issues. It means the company is structured to support rep performance, not just extract it.

Using Research in the Discovery Call

What You Found How to Use It
Founder has been building this company for 3 years "I saw you've been building this for three years — what's the trajectory looked like on the revenue side?"
Glassdoor reviews mention communication issues "What does communication between reps and leadership look like week to week on your team?"
Strong product reviews from customers Reference it in your Loom — "Your Trustpilot reviews show a pattern of customers who got real results. That tells me the offer can back up the close."
Active social content with a real following Ask about lead source: "I see you're active on YouTube — what percentage of your leads come from content versus paid?"

Do not interrogate during the discovery call. Your research informs your questions — it does not become a list of concerns you read aloud. Ask one or two specific, curious questions that show you did your homework. That positions you as the prepared, serious rep who evaluates partners — not the desperate applicant who just wants any role.

Lesson 6 · Section 13

Questions to Ask on the Offer Discovery Call

You are not there to impress them. You are there to evaluate a potential business partnership. That frame changes everything about how this call goes.

The Frame — You're Interviewing Them

Most reps go into an offer discovery call trying to convince the operator to hire them. That is the wrong orientation. You are evaluating whether this company is a good fit for you just as much as they are evaluating whether you are a good fit for them.

When you hold this frame, your questions become confident instead of tentative. You ask the hard ones. You do not flinch when they cannot answer. You treat vague answers as data. This is not arrogance — it is the behavior of someone who respects their own time and career enough to be selective.

Why This Frame Matters Practically

Operators can feel desperation. A rep who needs any role available asks soft questions or skips the hard ones entirely to avoid seeming demanding. A rep who is evaluating a business partnership asks the real questions and trusts that a good operator will respect the approach. The ones who do not are usually the ones with something to hide.

The 10 Questions Every Rep Must Ask

Question 01
"What is your current close rate, and who is generating it?"
Close rate is meaningless without context. 15% on warm inbound is different from 15% on cold outbound. Find out what the number is and what conditions are creating it.
Question 02
"What is the average deal size and commission structure?"
Get the specific number. Commission percentage. Whether it is flat fee or tiered. What the top-earning rep makes per month. You need the math to know if this is worth your time.
Question 03
"Where do leads come from and how many calls per week can I expect?"
This tells you your actual earning potential. 10% commission on 2 calls a week is poverty. 10% on 15 qualified inbound calls a week is a strong income. Volume and source matter as much as the rate.
Question 04
"What is your chargeback or refund rate, and how is it handled with reps?"
A high refund rate signals a product problem. And if the company deducts chargebacks from future commissions, you could be earning far less than the structure appears to offer. Know this upfront.
Question 05
"How long have your current reps been on the team?"
Rep tenure is one of the most honest data points available. Long tenure means reps are earning and being treated fairly. Short tenure or frequent turnover requires a real explanation.
Question 06
"What training or onboarding do you provide for new closers?"
Good operators have a ramp process. They want you to succeed. A company with zero onboarding structure puts all risk on the rep. Find out what support you get in the first 30 days.
Question 07
"What do your top reps make per month, and what does their pipeline look like?"
Ask for a real number, not a ceiling. Not "they can make up to $30K" but "our top rep made $18,400 last month. Here is what their call volume looked like." Real numbers or no deal.
Question 08
"What specific result does your product deliver for customers, and how do you track it?"
You need to believe in what you are closing. If the operator cannot articulate what customers get and how they measure it, you will have a hard time closing it with conviction. Conviction closes. Scripts don't.
Question 09
"Why did your last closer leave?"
This is the most revealing question on the list. Listen to how they answer it, not just what they say. Defensiveness, blame, or vagueness are all meaningful responses. A good operator answers this honestly and specifically.
Question 10
"What does a rep need to do to be successful here in the first 90 days?"
This surfaces expectations, structure, and whether the operator has thought carefully about rep performance. It also gives you a benchmark to hold yourself and them accountable to after you start.

What It Signals When They Can't Answer

Inability to answer the basic questions is itself an answer. An operator who has been running a successful sales operation for any meaningful period of time can cite their close rate, average deal size, and what their top rep earns. If they cannot answer three or more of these questions with specifics, the offer is either very new, poorly run, or actively misrepresented.

The Question That Reveals Everything
Rep
"Can you tell me what your current close rate is and what your top rep made last month?"
Operator (Red Flag Response)
"Well, it really depends on the rep. Our top reps are making really good money. We've had people do really well here. It's basically unlimited if you're good."
No close rate. No actual number. Deflects to rep quality. This is not an answer — it is avoidance. The rep should probe twice more. If still no specifics, walk away.
Operator (Green Flag Response)
"Sure. Current close rate is 14% on warm inbound. Our top closer made $17,200 last month. She ran about 18 calls. Average deal is $5,500 with a 10% commission structure."
Specific. Traceable. This operator knows their business. You can now evaluate whether the math works for you.
Lesson 7 · Section 13

Why Loom Works

A 60-second video does more than a three-paragraph email ever will. Not because it is more impressive — because it is a sales call. And sales is your job.

What Video Does That Text Can't

You are applying for a sales role. The operator's most important question is: can this person sell? A text application gives them credentials. A Loom gives them evidence.

Video communicates tone, confidence, presence, and energy simultaneously. None of those things exist in a resume or a LinkedIn message. When you record a Loom, you are not describing yourself as a sales professional. You are showing it, right now, in real time.

The Information Gap

A text application can say "I am a confident communicator with strong tonality." A Loom proves it or disproves it within the first five seconds. Operators who have hired before know this. They know a Loom is the closest thing to a discovery call before the discovery call. The ones who apply by text are putting themselves at a deliberate disadvantage.

What a Loom Demonstrates Before You've Said a Word

  • ✓
    Confidence. How you sit, how you open, whether you fidget or hold the frame. The first three seconds of a Loom communicate whether you are comfortable being seen. Closers need to be comfortable being seen.
  • ✓
    Preparation. A targeted Loom that references something specific about the company signals that you did research. That means you are someone who prepares before calls. Operators want reps who prepare.
  • ✓
    Communication clarity. Can you make a point quickly and without filler? The ability to be concise and specific in a Loom is the same ability required to run a clean sales call.
  • ✓
    Energy and tonality. The operator can hear whether you are alive and present or dull and scripted. Tonality is half the job. Your Loom is your audition for a role where tonality matters every single day.
  • ✓
    Initiative. You chose the harder format. That alone separates you from 80% of applicants before they hear a single word of your content.

The Conversion Differential

Response Rate Reality

Reps who apply with a targeted Loom get discovery call responses at dramatically higher rates than reps who apply with text messages or resume links. This is not a small margin. We are talking about the difference between 5-10% response rates on text applications and 40-60%+ response rates on strong Loom applications to quality offers.

The math is simple: if your goal is to land 1 discovery call from 10 applications, you can either send 10 text applications or 3-4 Loom applications with the same result. Loom saves time and gets better outcomes simultaneously.

The operators posting roles are often running high-ticket offers. They are used to seeing Zoom calls, video content, and video applications. A Loom is native to their world. A copy-paste text application is not. Match the medium to the audience.

Text Application Loom Application
Operator reads credentials and makes a guess about who you are Operator sees you in action — energy, tone, preparation, presence
Looks identical to every other text application in the inbox Stands out immediately as someone who does more than is asked
5-10% response rate in a competitive applicant pool 40-60%+ response rate when paired with research and specificity
Proves nothing about sales ability before the discovery call Is itself a mini sales call — demonstrates the core skill before a word is exchanged

Why Reps Avoid Loom (and What That Tells You)

The most common reason reps do not record a Loom: they do not trust themselves on camera. They are afraid of how they look. They do not know what to say. They feel awkward watching themselves. They think they will look bad compared to their imagination of what "good" looks like.

This is important to understand, because sales calls are on camera. Discovery calls are on camera. Closing calls are on camera. If a rep cannot bring themselves to record a 60-second video to apply for a role, that is the same gap that will show up on a call when a prospect pushes back or the rep feels exposed.

Avoiding Loom is not a logistics problem. It is a confidence signal. The rep who will not record one because they are afraid of how they look will also pull back on calls when they feel scrutinized. Close the gap here. The skills transfer directly.

"You're not applying for a job. You're evaluating a business partner. The rep who interviews the company gets the offer. The rep who begs for the offer doesn't."

— JJ Hernandez
Lesson 8 · Section 13

The 60-Second Loom Framework

Every application Loom follows the same four-part structure. Master this and every video you send is a sharp, confident, targeted pitch — not a nervous ramble.

The Four-Part Structure

60 seconds. Four parts. No filler. This is not a casual video — it is a compressed sales call where you are the product and the company is the prospect. Every second carries weight.

The structure: Hook, Credibility, Value Prop, CTA. In that order. Every time.

01
0:00 — 0:12
Hook — Why This Company
Open with something specific about this company. Not "I saw your posting and I'm interested." Reference something real: their product, their founder's content, a result a customer shared publicly, something you found in your research. This signals immediately that you are not sending a mass application. You chose them. That is rare.
"I watched your founder's breakdown of the psychology behind high-ticket closers last week and it matched exactly how I think about the sale. That's why I reached out specifically."
02
0:12 — 0:28
Credibility — Your Track Record
Give them one or two real, specific data points about your performance. Deal size you've closed. Close rate. Offer you worked on. Revenue generated. Named companies if relevant. Not a resume reading — two sentences that paint a picture of a rep who has produced results. If you are early in your career, cite what you can: a Sales.io mock call close rate, a previous role result, or a specific coaching skill you have been developing.
"I've closed $420K over the last 8 months on a B2C coaching offer at a 17% close rate. Before that, I did two years in agency sales where I consistently hit 110-130% of quota."
03
0:28 — 0:50
Value Prop — What You Bring to Their Specific Offer
Connect your experience to their specific offer. Why does your background make you a fit for what they are selling? What about their customer avatar lines up with prospects you have closed before? What specific skill or experience translates directly to their context? Make this connection explicitly. Do not leave them guessing.
"Your offer is in the mindset and transformation space, which is where I do my best work — customers buying for identity change respond well to the psychology-first approach I use, and your curriculum seems built around that same idea."
04
0:50 — 1:00
CTA — What You Want to Happen Next
Ask for a specific next step. Not "let me know if you're interested." Not "feel free to reach out." A confident, specific CTA: "I'd love 20 minutes to ask you a few questions about the offer structure and the pipeline. Are you open to a quick call this week?" The rep who asks directly for what they want is the rep who closes.
"I'd love a 20-minute discovery call with you to see if this is a mutual fit. I've got openings Thursday and Friday this week."

What Not to Say in Your Loom

Do not open with your name and why you are excited. "Hi, my name is [Name] and I am really excited to apply for this role" wastes the first 10 seconds and signals you are reading from a script. The hook is your opener. Get into it immediately.

Do not say "I am a hard worker" or "I am passionate about sales." These phrases mean nothing. Everyone says them. Replace them with a specific result. "I am a hard worker" becomes "I ran 22 calls last week and closed 4." Evidence replaces adjectives.

Do not go over 75 seconds. The 60-second constraint forces you to be specific. When reps remove the constraint, they ramble. A 3-minute Loom full of qualifications tells the operator you cannot be concise. That is a problem in sales, where every word on a call matters.

Do not use the same Loom for multiple companies. If your hook references "your company," but the word "your" does not correspond to anything specific, the operator hears a template. Templates do not get discovery calls.

Example Script Outline

This is a fill-in-the-blank outline, not a word-for-word script. Adapt every line to the specific company. Natural beats rehearsed every time.

The 60-Second Template
[0:00] "I came across [specific thing about the company — a piece of content, a result, their offer page]. That's what made me reach out directly."
One sentence. Specific. Shows you did the work.
[0:12] "Quick background on me: I've been in high-ticket sales for [X time], most recently on [offer type/vertical] closing [deal size] at [close rate or result]. Before that, [one other relevant data point]."
Two sentences. No resume list. Just the numbers that matter.
[0:28] "The reason I think there could be a strong fit here is [connection between your background and their specific offer, customer, or sales environment]."
This is the most important part. If you cannot make this connection specifically, go back to your research.
[0:50] "I'd love to schedule 20 minutes to ask you a few questions about the role and see if this is a mutual fit. I'm available [specific days]. Let me know."
Direct. Confident. Leaves no ambiguity about what you want next.

Record It, Watch It Once, Send It

The Process

Record the Loom. Watch it exactly once. If you stumbled badly in the first 5 seconds, re-record once. If it is an otherwise good take with a minor imperfection, send it. Perfection is not the goal. Authenticity and specificity are the goal. A rep who waited two hours to send the perfect Loom still loses to the rep who sent a solid one same day.

How Many Takes Is Too Many

Two. Three maximum if the first two were genuinely off. After that, you are in your head and the video will sound like it. What kills a Loom faster than a stumble is the flat, over-practiced energy of a rep who has recorded the same script 11 times and sounds robotic. Record it fresh. Send it.

Lesson 9 · Section 13

Loom Setup

Framing, lighting, audio, background. The video is your first impression — before you've said a word, they've already made a judgment. Get the setup right or the content doesn't matter.

Setup Is Credibility Before You Open Your Mouth

A grainy webcam shot with bad lighting signals low effort. That's the opposite of what a high-ticket closer should project. The prospect sees your video before they hear your pitch. If the visual quality reads as "I don't care about details," your application is done before you speak.

This doesn't require expensive gear. It requires attention. A $0 setup done well beats a $500 setup done carelessly.

The principle: Your Loom setup should communicate the same thing your call demeanor communicates — calm, prepared, intentional, professional. Nothing in the frame should work against that message.

The Four Setup Elements

📷
Framing
Floor: Camera at eye level. Not looking up at a laptop camera, not looking down from a monitor. Eye level. Your face should fill 55-65% of the frame — enough to see your expression clearly, not so close it's uncomfortable.
Pro level: Camera 18-24 inches from your face. Slight angle (not dead-on) adds depth. Rule of thirds if you want to be precise — eyes in the upper third of the frame.
💡
Lighting
Floor: Natural light source facing you. Sit facing a window — not with the window behind you (that makes you a silhouette). If no window, a lamp in front of you works. Warm light preferred over harsh overhead fluorescent.
Pro level: A ring light or soft box in front of you, slightly above eye level. $40-80 on Amazon. This alone upgrades the quality of your video significantly. Diffused light is more flattering than direct light.
🎙️
Audio
Floor: Wired earbuds with a microphone. Not laptop mic. The built-in laptop mic picks up keyboard, room echo, and background noise. Even the mic that comes with Apple EarPods is dramatically better than a laptop mic.
Pro level: A USB condenser mic ($60-100) or wireless lavalier clip-on mic. Clean audio signals care and professionalism — it's often the first thing prospects notice (consciously or not).
🧱
Background
Floor: A clean, uncluttered wall or a tidy room. No laundry in the background. No chaos. A plain white or neutral wall is completely fine — clean is professional.
Pro level: A tasteful background with intentional elements — a plant, a shelf with a few books, a Sales.io branded element. Something that says "I exist in a real workspace" without being distracting. Avoid virtual backgrounds — they look artificial on most setups.

Setup Mistakes That Kill Applications

The mistakeWhy it hurts
Looking up at the camera (laptop flat on desk)Makes you look small, the visual is unflattering, signals low effort
Window or light source behind youYour face goes dark, details disappear — you become unreadable
Using the laptop microphoneEchoey, hollow sound — makes you sound like you're in a bathroom
Busy or chaotic backgroundDistracts from your message, raises questions about your environment
Reading from a script off-screenEyes dart away constantly — you look unconfident and unreliable

The Pre-Send Check

Before sending any Loom: watch it back with the sound off, then with your eyes closed. Sound off: Does it look professional and credible? Eyes closed: Does your voice sound confident and clear? If both pass, send it.

A Loom that makes you cringe is not a Loom you should send. Record it again. You get better quickly. The reps who send imperfect Looms because "it's good enough" are leaving response rate on the table.

Lesson 10 · Section 13

The LinkedIn + Loom Combo

The message sequence that gets replies. Personalization in message one earns the open in message two. The Loom earns the call. Three messages that most hiring managers actually respond to.

Why the Sequence Matters

Sending a Loom in your first message is a mistake. The person doesn't know you, they didn't ask for a video, and they're not going to click a link from someone they've never engaged with. You need to earn the watch before you ask for it.

The sequence earns the right to the Loom. Message 1 is human and specific — it gets a response. Message 2 delivers the Loom inside a context where they're already open to hearing from you.

The principle is identical to the sales call: Earn the right before you present. You wouldn't pitch in the first minute of a discovery call. Don't pitch in the first message of a DM sequence either.

The 3-Message Sequence

Message 1 — Connect and be specific
Hey [First name] — saw you're building out [specific thing you found on their profile / content / company]. Looks like you're going after [market or ICP they're targeting]. Love the approach.

I'm a closer who's been trained in scriptless high-ticket sales. Would love to learn more about what you're building — would a quick conversation make sense?
Keep it under 80 words. The specificity is the key — it proves you actually looked at them, not just blasted a template. No pitch. No mention of the Loom. Just genuine curiosity.
Message 2 — Deliver the Loom (after they reply)
Appreciate the response. I put together a quick 60-second video on why I think I'd be a strong fit for what you're building — rather than just send a resume, I figured you'd get more from actually hearing me.

[Loom link]

Curious what you think. Happy to jump on a call if it seems worth exploring.
The framing matters: "rather than just send a resume" — positions the Loom as a better choice, not just an outreach tactic. Keep this message short. The Loom does the work.
Message 3 — If no reply to the Loom after 2-3 days
Just wanted to make sure the video didn't get buried — [Loom link] — around 60 seconds. No pressure either way. Just think there could be something worth a quick conversation about.
Light, non-needy, confident. One follow-up on the Loom. If there's no reply after this, move to the standard follow-up protocol (see Lesson 11).

Finding the Right Person to Message

  • For smaller offers (founder-led): Message the founder directly. Search the company name on LinkedIn, look at the team, find the person with "Founder," "CEO," or "Head of Sales" in the title.
  • For larger companies: Go to "Sales Director," "VP of Sales," or "Head of Closers." Not HR — they don't know what they're looking for in a closer and they can't evaluate you.
  • Read their profile before you message. Pull one specific thing — a post they made, a result they mentioned, something about the offer. Generic first messages get ignored. The specificity is the entire reason this sequence works.

Timing and Cadence

Don't message 20 companies in one day. You can't write genuinely personalized first messages at that volume — and generic messages get ignored. Three to five genuine, specific outreaches per day is more productive than twenty templated ones. Quality compounds. Noise doesn't.

Best days to send Message 1: Tuesday through Thursday, morning. People check LinkedIn in the morning and on weekdays. Monday morning is often too busy. Friday afternoon is too close to the weekend.

Lesson 11 · Section 13

Following Up After the Loom

Timing, tone, and how many times. The follow-up is where most reps either quit too early or go so hard they burn the relationship. Here's the protocol.

Why Most People Don't Reply Immediately

People who are interested but didn't reply usually have one of three things happening: they got busy and it slipped, they watched your Loom but wanted to "get back to it later" and forgot, or they need a reason to feel like it's worth their time to engage.

A follow-up gives them permission to re-engage without the awkwardness of "sorry I missed this." It also signals persistence — which, in the sales world, is a quality they're specifically looking for.

The tone of your follow-up IS your sample of how you'd handle prospect objections on their calls. If you follow up desperately, you're demonstrating exactly what you'll do when a prospect ghosts after a call. They see this. Follow up like a confident closer, not a needy applicant.

The Follow-Up Sequence — 3 Touches Maximum

Touch 1 — Loom reminder
2-3 days after Loom
"Just wanted to make sure this didn't get buried — [Loom link]. Around 60 seconds. No pressure on timing."
Short, light, non-needy. Just making it easy for them to engage if they want to.
Touch 2 — Add value or update
5-7 days after Touch 1
"I know you're busy — just wanted to follow up one more time. I've been looking at what [company] is doing with [specific angle] and think there's a real opportunity to accelerate [outcome they're going after]. If there's a window for a quick call, I'd love to hear what you're building."
This follow-up adds something — a specific observation about their business. It signals you're still paying attention and that you've done your homework.
Touch 3 — The graceful exit
5-7 days after Touch 2
"I'll stop reaching out after this — I don't want to crowd your inbox. If the timing ever works, I'd love to connect. I think we'd work well together."
The graceful exit often generates a reply. It's confident, it's final, and it respects their time. If they don't reply to this, let it go and move on.

What Kills the Follow-Up

Needy follow-up (kills it)Confident follow-up (works)
"Hey, just wanted to check in for the third time — did you get a chance to watch my video?""Just wanted to make sure this didn't get buried."
Apologizing for following upNo apology — it's a normal and professional thing to do
Sending the 5th or 6th follow-up with increasing desperation3 touches max, then graceful release
Making them feel guilty for not respondingGiving them an easy on-ramp back into the conversation
Following up on multiple channels simultaneouslyStaying in the channel where you connected initially

When to Move On

After 3 touches with no reply: move on. Not because they're a bad person — because they're not the right fit right now. There are dozens of offers on the board. The time you spend chasing one non-responder is time away from finding an offer that's actually ready to move.

Keep the door open. Leave them on a positive note. Markets are small. The hiring manager who doesn't respond today might be a decision maker on a future offer you want to join. How you exit matters as much as how you entered.

Lesson 12 · Section 13

The Offer Interview

You're not applying for a job. You're evaluating a business partner. The rep who interviews the company gets the offer. The rep who begs for the offer doesn't.

The Frame That Changes Everything

Most reps go into an interview in supplicant mode: "Please hire me, I'll be great, I promise." This is the worst frame you can bring to a sales interview — because they can see it, and they know exactly what it means on a sales call.

The rep who performs in an interview interview is performing. Elite closers show their character by how they conduct themselves — not by trying to impress. The frame of mutual evaluation changes everything.

The reframe: You're both evaluating fit. They're evaluating whether you can close their offer. You're evaluating whether their offer is worth closing. Both sides have standards. Both sides get to say no. This frame, genuinely held, makes you magnetic.

How to Sell Yourself Without Sounding Desperate

DesperateConfident
"I can close anything, I'm a fast learner, I'll do whatever it takes.""My strongest close rates have been on [specific type of offer]. I'm looking for something where the product actually delivers — because my long-term close rate depends on that."
Immediately agreeing with everything they say about the offerAsking specific questions about their current close rate and why the last closer left
Not mentioning your own results because you're afraid they won't be good enoughLeading with your real numbers, explaining what drove them, and what you're working to improve
Saying yes to any commission structure without understanding it"Walk me through how commission is calculated — specifically what happens if a payment fails."

The Interview in Practice

Confident mutual evaluation — what it sounds like
Hiring managerSo tell me about yourself and why you're interested in this role.
RepHappy to. I've been in high-ticket sales for about 18 months — most of my experience has been on coaching and education offers in the $5k-$15k range. I've consistently closed in the 20-25% range. Right now I'm looking for an offer where the product genuinely delivers, because my close rate is directly tied to the product's reputation. I'd love to hear — what does your current close rate look like with your top closers?
Led with real numbers, stated what they're looking for, immediately turned it into a mutual evaluation.
Hiring managerWe're around 18% average, top closer is at 28%.
RepThat's a healthy spread. What's the main difference between your top closer and the average — is it discovery, objection handling, or something else?
Asked a question that only someone who knows sales would ask. Continues the mutual evaluation — now they're being interviewed.

Questions to Ask in the Interview

These questions do two things simultaneously: they give you the information you need to evaluate the offer, and they demonstrate that you're a serious, informed rep who knows the right questions to ask.

1. What's your current close rate across the team? What's your top closer doing?
2. What's the average deal size? What's the split pay structure?
3. How is commission paid — weekly, monthly? What's the chargeback policy?
4. What does the lead pipeline look like — where does most of your traffic come from?
5. What happened with the last closer who left? Why did they leave?
6. What does a typical booked call look like — what kind of prospect?
7. What support does the closing team have — setter handoff notes, call recordings, coaching?

If they can't answer questions 1-3 with specific numbers, that's a red flag. Legitimate offers track their numbers. If a company can't tell you their close rate, they either don't know it (bad) or are hiding it (worse).

Lesson 13 · Section 13

Your First 30 Days on a New Offer

How you ramp determines your ceiling on the offer. Most reps try to close before they're ready and blow their trust with the team. Here's how to ramp fast, the right way.

The Principle Behind Ramping Fast

Ramping fast doesn't mean getting on calls immediately and hoping. It means front-loading the inputs so that when you're on calls, you're already operating from internalized knowledge — not guessing.

The fastest ramp is the one where you've done enough pre-work that the first call doesn't feel like a learning experience. It feels like a deployment of what you've already prepared.

The top question to answer before call 1: Can you explain the offer as if you were one of their best customers describing how it changed your life? If yes, you're ready. If not, more product immersion needed.

Week by Week

Days 1-7 Product immersion — become the customer before you sell to them
Know the product story: Every module, every framework, every transformation. Know what it actually does for the people who use it.
Know the customer avatar: Read every testimonial. Who are the people who buy? What was their life before? What changed? The more specifically you know this, the more precisely you can speak to a prospect who matches.
Know the objections: What are the top 5 reasons people don't buy? What are the best responses? Ask the team. Review past call recordings if they're available.
Know the numbers: Average close rate, average deal size, split pay prevalence, chargeback rate. You need these to set your own targets and to answer prospect questions confidently.
Days 8-14 Shadow and practice — watch, then drill
Shadow at least 2 calls from the top closer if possible. Watch how they run discovery. Notice the specific questions they ask and what they do with the answers.
Run at least 3 mock calls with another rep or your coach. Escalating difficulty — first an easy prospect, then a difficult one, then a frame attack drill.
Know the pitch cold. Not scripted — internalized. You should be able to pitch the offer from any angle the prospect's discovery has revealed.
Days 15-21 First real calls — debrief everything
Do a 5-minute debrief after every call. What happened, what worked, what broke. Write it down. Don't move to the next call without doing this.
Record every call and listen back to at least one per day. Look for the moment the call shifted — good or bad — and identify what you did at that moment.
